title: EXPOSÉ III B CALCULATIONS OF LOCAL TERMS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ III B CALCULATIONS OF LOCAL TERMS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ III B
CALCULATIONS OF LOCAL TERMSby Luc Illusie

This exposé, written in January 1977, does not correspond to any oral exposé of the seminar. It consists of two independent parts. In the first, we compute the Lefschetz-Verdier local terms for cohomological correspondences between smooth curves over an algebraically closed field, with support in correspondences satisfying certain general position hypotheses. The formula we obtain yields as corollaries the formula of Verdier [6] for transversal endomorphisms (which applies in particular to the case of the Frobenius endomorphism), and the formula stated by Langlands in ([4] 7.12). The method of proof is parallel to that followed by Artin-Verdier in [6]. We reduce to proving the vanishing of the local term when the given complexes of sheaves have zero stalk at the images of the fixed point under consideration, and we prove in fact a stronger property, namely that a certain restriction arrow is zero (2.5). To establish this property, we begin by reducing to the case of tame ramification by means of coverings of the same degree of the two given curves (in order not to destroy the general position hypotheses), then we treat this case directly with the help of Abhyankar's lemma and the relative purity theorem. The second part of this exposé, of a much more technical nature, is inspired by the method used by Grothendieck to establish the Lefschetz formula for certain cohomological correspondences on curves (see XII and (SGA 41/2 Rapport)). In n° 5, we develop, for complexes of modules on a topos, a theory of non-commutative traces which generalizes the commutative theory of (SGA 6 I) and gives back, in the case of a punctual topos, the non-commutative theory developed by Grothendieck in the oral seminar (the latter had been written up in exposé XI, unfortunately lost). We apply the techniques of n° 5 to define, in n° 6, Lefschetz-Verdier local terms for cohomological correspondences between complexes of modules over

not necessarily commutative rings. These local terms behave like non-commutative traces, and make it possible in particular, in the case where one takes as base ring the algebra of a group, to divide canonically the ordinary local terms attached to equivariant correspondences. As an application, we prove Grothendieck's divisibility conjecture of (XII 4.5), in a more general form. We also show that the local terms defined by Grothendieck in the Lefschetz formula of XII are indeed the Lefschetz-Verdier local terms, and we generalize the formula of (loc. cit.).

I thank P. Deligne for the useful suggestions he made to me in the course of numerous conversations; I am particularly grateful to him for having pointed out to me, and helped me correct, an error in an earlier proof of theorem 1.2.

pages.title: I. — Correspondences in general position between curves.

blocks.html: I. — Correspondences in general position between curves.

In this part, \(S\) denotes the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\) of char. \(p\), Λ a noetherian commutative ring killed by an integer prime to \(p\). For every scheme \(X\) over \(S\) we will denote by \(D(X)\) the derived category of sheaves of Λ-modules on \(X\), and by \(D_{ctf}(X)\) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) formed of the complexes of finite tor-dimension with constructible cohomology.

pages.title: 1. — Statement of the theorem and corollaries.

blocks.html: 1. — Statement of the theorem and corollaries.
1.1. Let \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) be an \(S\)-scheme which is the strict localization of a smooth and separated \(S\)-curve at a closed point, \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) a strictly local \(S\)-scheme,
\[ a:A\longrightarrow X\times_S Y,\qquad b:B\longrightarrow X\times_S Y \]

\(S\)-morphisms. We denote by \(p_1:X\times_S Y\longrightarrow X\), \(p_2:X\times_S Y\longrightarrow Y\) the projections, \(a_i=p_i a\), \(b_i=p_i b\), and by \(x\) (resp. \(y\)) the closed point of \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)). We assume \(a_2\) and \(b_1\) finite and surjective, and \(A\times_{(X\times_S Y)}B\) reduced to the closed point \(z\) with image \((x,y)\) in \(X\times_S Y\).
Let \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\). Denote by
\[ u_z\in\operatorname{Hom}(L_x,M_y)\qquad (\text{resp. }v_z\in\operatorname{Hom}(M_y,L_x)) \]
the composite homomorphism
\[ L_x=R\Gamma(X,L)\xrightarrow{a_1^*}R\Gamma(A,a_1^*L) \xrightarrow{R\Gamma(A,u)}R\Gamma(A,a_2^!M) \xrightarrow{a_{2*}}R\Gamma(Y,M)=M_y, \]

where \(a_{2*}\) is defined by the adjunction arrow \(a_{2*}a_2^!M\longrightarrow M\) (resp. the analogous composite homomorphism defined with the help of \((b,v)\)). Since \(L_x\) and \(M_y\) are perfect complexes of Λ-modules, we can form the cup-product (SGA 6 I 8.3)
\[ (1.1.1)\qquad \langle u_z,v_z\rangle=\operatorname{Tr}(u_zv_z) =\operatorname{Tr}(v_zu_z)\in\Lambda. \]

Set ΛX(1)[2] = \(K_X\), ΛY(1)[2] = \(K_Y\). By (I 5) (or (SGA 41/2 Dualité 1, or Finitude 4.1)) the functor \(D_X=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,K_X)\) (resp. \(D_Y=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,K_Y)\)) on

\(D_{ctf}(X)\) (resp. \(D_{ctf}(Y)\)) is dualizing, i.e. for every \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\) (resp. \(D_{ctf}(Y)\)), we have \(E\xrightarrow{\sim}D_XD_XE\) (resp. \(E\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YD_YE\)). We will sometimes write \(D\) instead of \(D_X\), \(D_Y\). On the other hand denote by \(p_i^!\) the functor \(p_i^*(-)(1)[2]\). We trivially have
\[ K_X\overset L\otimes_S M\xrightarrow{\sim}p_2^!M,\qquad L\overset L\otimes_S K_Y\xrightarrow{\sim}p_1^!L, \]
and it follows from (III 2.3), by passage to the limit, that the Künneth arrows analogous to (III 3.1.1)
\[ DL\overset L\otimes_S M\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L,p_2^!M),\qquad L\overset L\otimes_S DM\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_2^*M,p_1^!L) \]

are isomorphisms. Since \(a_2\) is finite, \(a\) is finite, so the functor \(a^!\) is defined, and we verify easily that we have the transitivity isomorphism \(a^!p_2^!=a_2^!\). Thanks to the induction formula for \(a\) (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.2), we therefore have a canonical isomorphism analogous to (III 3.2.1)
\[ a^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L,p_2^!M) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M), \]
whence an isomorphism
\[ (*)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(X\times_S Y,a_*a^!(DL\overset L\otimes_S M)). \]
We similarly have an isomorphism
\[ (**)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(X\times_S Y,b_*b^!(L\overset L\otimes_S DM)). \]
We define, as in (III 4.1.2), a natural pairing
\[ (DL\overset L\otimes_S M)\overset L\otimes (L\overset L\otimes_S DM)\longrightarrow K_X\overset L\otimes_S K_Y=\Lambda_{X\times_S Y}(2)[4] \xrightarrow{\mathrm{dfn}}K_{X\times_S Y}. \]
By composition with the pairing analogous to (III 4.2.3)
\[ a_*a^!P\overset L\otimes b_*b^!Q\longrightarrow c_*c^!(P\overset L\otimes Q), \]

where \(P=DL\overset L\otimes_S M\), \(Q=L\overset L\otimes_S DM\), and \(c:z=A\times_{(X\times_S Y)}B\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\) is the canonical projection, and taking into account the isomorphisms (*), (**), we deduce from it a pairing analogous to (III 4.2.5)
\[ \langle\ ,\ \rangle:\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\longrightarrow H^0(X\times_S Y,c_*c^!K_{X\times_S Y}). \]
We verify easily, by passage to the limit, that \(c^!K_{X\times_S Y}=\Lambda_z\), so that \(H^0(X\times_S Y,c_*c^!K_{X\times_S Y})=\Lambda\). We therefore have at our disposal a "cup-product"
\[ (1.1.2)\qquad \langle u,v\rangle\in\Lambda. \]

1.1.3 Let \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)) be a smooth \(S\)-curve, \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)) a separated \(S\)-curve, \(a':A'\longrightarrow X'\times_S Y'\), \(b':B'\longrightarrow X'\times_S Y'\) finite \(S\)-morphisms, \(x'\) (resp. \(y'\)) a closed point of \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)), \(z'\) an isolated closed point of \(A'\times_{(X'\times_S Y')}B'\), with image \(s'\) (resp. \(t'\)) in \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)), and such that \(a_2'(=p_2a')\) (resp. \(b_1'(=p_1b')\)) is quasi-finite at \(s'\) (resp. \(t'\)),
\[ L'\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X'),\quad M'\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y'),\quad u'\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1'^*L',a_2'^!M'),\quad v'\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2'^*M',b_1'^!L'). \]

We can then consider the local term (III 4.2.7) \(\langle u',v'\rangle_{z'}\in\Lambda\). On the other hand, if \(X\), \(Y\), \(A\), \(B\) are the strict localizations of \(X'\), \(Y'\), \(A'\), \(B'\) at \(x'\), \(y'\), \(s'\), \(t'\), \(a:A\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\), \(b:B\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\) the morphisms deduced from \(a'\), \(b'\), \(L=L'|X\), \(M=M'|Y\), and \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\) the arrows deduced from \(u'\), \(v'\), the data \((X,Y,a,b,u,v)\) satisfy the hypotheses of the beginning of 1.1, so a cup-product 1.1.2 \(\langle u,v\rangle\in\Lambda\) is defined. It follows from the compatibility of the formation of the local terms (III 4.2.7) with localization (III 4.2.6) that we have \(\langle u',v'\rangle_{z'}=\langle u,v\rangle\). It is easy to see, moreover, that every datum \((X,Y,a,b,u,v)\) satisfying the hypotheses of the beginning of 1.1 is induced by a datum \((X',Y',a',b',x',y',z',s',t',u',v')\) as above, where we may assume in addition, if desired, that \(A'\times_{(X'\times_S Y')}B'\) is reduced to \(z'\).
In general, the cup-products 1.1.1 and 1.1.2 are unequal. We have however the following theorem, which is the main result of this first part:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2

Under the hypotheses of the beginning of 1.1, we assume in addition that \(A\) and \(B\) are in general position, i.e. that the intersection of the tangent cones at \((x,y)\) to the images of \(A\) and \(B\) in \(X\times_S Y\) is reduced to \((x,y)\), and that similarly \(A\) and \(X\times\{y\}\) are in general position, as well as \(\{x\}\times Y\) and \(B\). Then we have:

\[ (1.2.1)\qquad \langle u_z,v_z\rangle=\langle u,v\rangle. \]
By the Lefschetz-Verdier formula (III 4.7), taking 1.1.3 into account, we deduce from it:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

Let \(F\) be an endomorphism of a proper and smooth curve \(X/S\) such that the scheme of fixed points \(X^F\) is finite and formed of points where the graph of \(F\) is transverse to the diagonal. Let \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), and \(uinoperatorname{Hom}(F^*L,L)\). Then we have

\[ \operatorname{Tr}((F,u),R\Gamma(X,L))= \sum_{x\in X^F}\operatorname{Tr}(u_x,L_x), \]

where the trace, on both sides, is taken in the sense of (SGA 6 I 8).

For \(L\) concentrated in degree 0, 1.3 is the result of Artin-Verdier ([6] 4.1). By a passage to the limit explained in XV, we deduce from 1.3 an analogous result in ℓ-adic cohomology ([6] 1.1), (XV §2 n° 3 prop. 2):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.1

With \(X\) and \(F\) as in 1.3, let \(L\) be a constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf on \(X\) (ℓ prime ≠ \(p\)), and \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L,L)\). Then we have:

\[ \sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}((F,u),H^i(X,L))= \sum_{x\in X^F}\operatorname{Tr}(u_x,L_x). \]
The hypotheses of 1.3, 1.3.1 apply in particular to the case where \((F,u)\) is a Frobenius correspondence (XV 2), whence the trace formula of Grothendieck ([2] 5.1), (SGA 41/2 Rapport 3.2).
1.4. With the notations of 1.1, suppose \(b_2\) and \(a_1\) finite and surjective (instead of \(a_2\) and \(b_1\)), and consider the dual correspondences (III 5.1)
\[ Du\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_2^*DM,a_1^!DL),\qquad Dv\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_1^*DL,b_2^!DM), \]
and the homomorphisms
\[ (Du)_z\in\operatorname{Hom}((DM)_y,(DM)_x),\qquad (Dv)_z\in\operatorname{Hom}((DL)_x,(DM)_y). \]
Suppose \((A,\{x\}\times Y)\) in general position, as well as \((X\times\{y\},B)\), \((A,B)\). Applying 1.2 to (Du,Dv), we obtain:
\[ \langle Du,Dv\rangle=\langle(Du)_z,(Dv)_z\rangle, \]
whence, by (III 5.1.6) and (III 4.2.6),
\[ (1.4.1)\qquad \langle u,v\rangle=\langle(Du)_z,(Dv)_z\rangle. \]
From this remark and from 1.2 we deduce, by the Lefschetz-Verdier formula (III 4.7):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let \(X,Y\) be proper and smooth curves over \(S\),

\[ a:A\longrightarrow X\times_S Y,\qquad b:B\longrightarrow X\times_S Y \]

correspondences where \(A\) and \(B\) are proper curves over \(S\), \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\),

\[ u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M),\qquad v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L). \]

We assume that \(C=A\mathbin{\times_{(X\times_S Y)}}B\) is finite over \(S\) and of the form \(C=C_1\amalg C_2\), with the following properties: for \(z\in C_1\), the morphisms deduced from \(a_2,b_1\) by strict localization at the images of \(z\) are finite, and, if \((x,y)\) denotes the image of \(z\) in \(X\times_S Y\), each of the pairs \((A,B)\), \((A,X\times\{y\})\), \((\{x\}\times Y,B)\) is in general position at \((x,y)\) ; for \(z\in C_2\), the morphisms deduced from \(a_1,b_2\) by strict localization at the images of \(z\) are finite, and each of the pairs \((A,B)\), \((A,\{x\}\times Y)\), \((B,X\times\{y\})\) is in general position at \((x,y)\) ("general position" meaning that the intersection of the tangent cones at \((x,y)\) to the images of the curves of the pair under consideration is reduced to \((x,y)\)). Then, if

\[ u_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma(X,L),R\Gamma(Y,M)),\qquad v_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma(Y,M),R\Gamma(X,L)) \]

are the morphisms defined by \(u,v\) (III 3.7.6), we have, with the notations of 1.1.1, (III 5.1.5),

\[ (1.5.1)\qquad \langle u_*,v_*\rangle= \sum_{z\in C_1}\langle u_z,v_z\rangle+ \sum_{z\in C_2}\langle(Du)_z,(Dv)_z\rangle. \]

1.6. Assuming the formalism of ℓ-adic cohomology, we deduce from 1.5, by the passage-to-the-limit technique of XIV, that, under the hypotheses of 1.5 on \(X,Y,a,b\), we have, for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) (ℓ prime ≠ \(p\)), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell)\),
\[ u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M),\qquad v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L), \]

the equality analogous to 1.5.1 in \(\mathbb Q_\ell\). This formula contains as a special case proposition 7.12 of Langlands [4]: apply the formula with \(X=Y\), \(M\) the sheaf \(F\) considered by Langlands, \(L=\alpha M\) \((\alpha\in\mathbb Q_\ell^*)\), \(A=\) the diagonal of \(X\times X\), \(b=\) the correspondence φ of (loc. cit.)

\(u=\) the correspondence with support in \(A\) given by \(\alpha^{-1}:L\longrightarrow M\), \(v=\) the correspondence φ of (loc. cit.); with the notations of (loc. cit.), \(C_1\) (resp. \(C_2\)) is the set of fixed points where \(a<d\) (resp. \(a>d\)).
1.7. Under the hypotheses of 1.2, take for \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) the correspondence \(cl(A)\) (resp. \(cl(B)\)) defined by
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{a_2}:a_{2*}\Lambda\longrightarrow\Lambda \qquad(\text{resp. }\operatorname{Tr}_{b_1}:b_{1*}\Lambda\longrightarrow\Lambda). \]
Formula 1.2.1 reads
\[ (1.7.1)\qquad \deg(a_2)\deg(b_1)=\langle cl(A),cl(B)\rangle, \]

where \(\deg(a_2)\) (resp. \(\deg(b_1)\)) denotes the generic degree of \(a_2\) (resp. \(b_1\)). Taking into account the general position hypotheses, the left-hand side of 1.7.1 is none other than the intersection multiplicity at \((x,y)\) of the cycles \(a_*A\), \(b_*B\), direct images of \(A\) and \(B\). It is not difficult to deduce 1.7.1 directly from the compatibility of the formation of the cohomology class associated with a cycle with intersections (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3.8).
2. — Reduction to a vanishing theorem.

2.1. Under the hypotheses of 1.1, denote by \(s\) (resp. \(t\)) the image of \(z\) in \(A\) (resp. \(B\)), and by \(i:U=X-\{x\}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) and \(j:V=Y-\{y\}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow Y\) the canonical inclusions. Since \(a_2\) and \(b_1\) are finite, we have \(a_2^{-1}(y)=s\), \(b_1^{-1}(x)=t\), hence \(a_1^{-1}(U)\subset a_2^{-1}(V)\), \(b_2^{-1}(V)\subset b_1^{-1}(U)\), and we have commutative diagrams

\[ \xymatrix{ U & a_1^{-1}(U)\ar[l]_{a'_1}\ar[r]^{a'_2}\ar[d] & V\ar[d]^j\\ X\ar[u]^i & A\ar[l]^{a_1}\ar[r]_{a_2} & Y }\qquad (1)\quad(2), \] \[ \xymatrix{ U & b_2^{-1}(V)\ar[l]_{b'_1}\ar[r]^{b'_2}\ar[d] & V\ar[d]^j\\ X\ar[u]^i & B\ar[l]^{b_1}\ar[r]_{b_2} & Y }\qquad (3)\quad(4), \]
where the squares (1) and (4) are cartesian.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

To prove 1.2, we may restrict ourselves to considering the following two cases:

a) \(L_x=0, M_y=0\) ; b) \(L|U=0, M|V=0\).
Consider the decreasing filtrations on \(L,M\) defined by the subcomplexes

\[ F^nL=\begin{cases} L&\text{if }n\leq0,\\ i_!i^*L&\text{if }n=1,\\ 0&\text{if }n>1, \end{cases} \qquad F^nM=\begin{cases} M&\text{if }n\leq0,\\ j_!j^*M&\text{if }n=1,\\ 0&\text{if }n>1. \end{cases} \]

The complex \(a_{2*}a_1^*L\) (resp. \(b_{1*}b_2^*M\)) is then filtered by \(F^na_{2*}a_1^*L=a_{2*}a_1^*F^nL\). We have \(F^na_{2*}a_1^*L=a_{2*}a_1^*L\) for \(n\leq0\), and 0 for \(n>1\) ; moreover, since \(a_2\) is finite and the square (1) cartesian, we have
We similarly have
\[ F^nb_{1*}b_2^*M= \begin{cases} b_{1*}b_2^*M&\text{if }n\leq0,\\ i_!(b'_1)_!(b'_2)^*j^*M&\text{if }n=1,\\ 0&\text{if }n>1. \end{cases} \]
We may assume \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) defined by a homomorphism of complexes
\[ u:a_{2*}a_1^*L\longrightarrow M \qquad(\text{resp. }v:b_{1*}b_2^*M\longrightarrow L). \]
The formulas above show that the composite
\[ F^1a_{2*}a_1^*L\longrightarrow a_{2*}a_1^*L\xrightarrow{u}M \quad (\text{resp. }F^1b_{1*}b_2^*M\longrightarrow b_{1*}b_2^*M\xrightarrow{v}L) \]

factors (in a unique manner) through \(F^1M\) (resp. \(F^1L\)), in other words that \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) is a homomorphism of filtered complexes. Thus \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) underlies a filtered correspondence (in the sense of (III 4.13)), which we will still denote
\[ u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{DF}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M) \qquad(\text{resp. }v\in\operatorname{Hom}_{DF}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)). \]
By the additivity of cup-products ([3] V 3.8.7), (III 4.13.1), we have

\[ \langle u_z,v_z\rangle= \langle\operatorname{gr}^0u_z,\operatorname{gr}^0v_z\rangle+ \langle\operatorname{gr}^1u_z,\operatorname{gr}^1v_z\rangle, \] \[ \langle u,v\rangle= \langle\operatorname{gr}^0u,\operatorname{gr}^0v\rangle+ \langle\operatorname{gr}^1u,\operatorname{gr}^1v\rangle. \]

The lemma follows, for \(\operatorname{gr}^1L=i_!i^*L\) (resp. \(\operatorname{gr}^1M=j_!j^*M\)) has zero stalk at \(x\) (resp. \(y\)), while \(\operatorname{gr}^0L\) (resp. \(\operatorname{gr}^0M\)) is concentrated at \(x\) (resp. \(y\)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

Under the hypotheses of 1.1, suppose \(L|U=0\), \(M|V=0\). Then we have \(\langle u_z,v_z\rangle=\langle u,v\rangle\).

The idea of the proof is that then \(u\) and \(v\) are direct images of correspondences between complexes concentrated at the closed points and that the formula to be established merely expresses the Lefschetz formula for the projections \(s\longrightarrow S\), \(y\longrightarrow S\). Taking into account 1.1.3, we may replace the data of 1.1 by the following: \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) is a smooth \(S\)-curve equipped with a closed point \(x\) (resp. \(y\)), \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) is a separated \(S\)-curve, \(a:A\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\), \(b:B\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\) are finite \(S\)-morphisms such that \(A\mathbin{\times_{(X\times_S Y)}}B\) is reduced to a closed point \(z\) above \((x,y)\),
\[ u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M),\qquad v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L), \]
with \(L=h_{1*}L'\), \(M=h_{2*}M'\), \(L'\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(\{x\})\), \(M'\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(\{y\})\),

\(h_1:\{x\}\longrightarrow X\) and \(h_2:\{y\}\longrightarrow Y\) denoting the canonical inclusions. We must show that \(\langle u_z,v_z\rangle=\langle u,v\rangle_z\), where \(u_z\in\operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma(X,L),R\Gamma(Y,M))\), \(v_z\in\operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma(Y,M),R\Gamma(X,L))\) are defined as in 1.1, and \(\langle u,v\rangle_z\) is the value at \(z\) of the cup-product (III 4.2.7). We have \(R\Gamma(X,L)=R\Gamma_c(X,L)=L_x\), \(R\Gamma(Y,M)=R\Gamma_c(Y,M)=M_y\), and \(u_z\) (resp. \(v_z\)) is none other than the direct image \(u_*\) (resp. \(v_*\)) of \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) on \(S\) in the sense of (III 3.7.6), as the computation procedure (III 3.5 b)) shows. On the other hand, if \(h=h_1\times_S h_2:(x,y)\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\) is the inclusion, and if \(a':A'\longrightarrow(x,y)\), \(b':B'\longrightarrow(x,y)\) denote the fibers of \(a,b\) at \((x,y)\) (each reduced to a point), we have canonical isomorphisms

\[h_*a'_*a'^!(DL_x\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M_y)\xrightarrow{\sim}a_*a^!(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M),\] \[h_*b'_*b'^!(L_x\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DM_y)\xrightarrow{\sim}b_*b^!(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DM),\]
and the arrows

\[h_*:\operatorname{Hom}(a_1'^*L_x,a_2'^!M_y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M),\] \[h_*:\operatorname{Hom}(b_2'^*M_y,b_1'^!L_x)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\quad\text{(III 3.7.6)}\]
are isomorphisms. The correspondences \(u\) and \(v\) can therefore be written (in a unique manner)
\[(1)\qquad u=h_*u',\qquad v=h_*v',\]

with \(u'\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1'^*L_x,a_2'^!M_y)\), \(v'\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2'^*M_y,b_1'^!L_x)\). By the Lefschetz formula (III 4.5.1) for \((h_1,h_2,A'\longrightarrow A,B'\longrightarrow B)\), we have
\[(2)\qquad\langle u,v\rangle_z=\langle u',v'\rangle_z.\]
On the other hand, we have, by (1), \(u_*=u'_*\), \(v_*=v'_*\), whence, by the Lefschetz formula for \(\{x\}\longrightarrow S\), \(\{y\}\longrightarrow S\),
\[(3)\qquad\langle u_*,v_*\rangle=\langle u',v'\rangle_z.\]
Taking into account the remark made above, the conclusion follows from combining (2) and (3).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4

To prove 1.2, we may assume that \(a\) and \(b\) are closed immersions, and it suffices to prove that, if \(L_x=0\), \(M_y=0\), the restriction arrow

\[(2.4.1)\qquad H_A^0(X\times_S Y,DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M)\longrightarrow H_z^0(B,b^*(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M))\]

is zero.

Set \(X\times_S Y=Z\). The closed subschemes \(a':A'\longrightarrow Z\), \(b':B'\longrightarrow Z\), images of \(a\) and \(b\), satisfy the same hypotheses as \(a\) and \(b\). Let \(u'\) (resp. \(v'\)) be the correspondence with support in \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)) which is the direct image of \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) (by the arrow deduced from the adjunction arrow \(f_*f^!\longrightarrow1\) (resp. \(g_*g^!\longrightarrow1\)) where \(f:A\longrightarrow A'\) (resp. \(g:B\longrightarrow B'\)) is the projection). It is immediate that \(u_z=u'_z\), \(v_z=v'_z\). On the other hand, it follows easily from the definition of the cup-product 1.1.2 that \(\langle u,v\rangle=\langle u',v'\rangle\), whence the first assertion. By 2.2 and 2.3, we may assume that \(L_x=0\), \(M_y=0\), and the conclusion of 1.2 then reads \(\langle u,v\rangle=0\). Let \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Z)\). The cup-product
\[H_A^0(Z,P)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}H_B^0(Z,Q)\longrightarrow H_z^0(Z,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\]
decomposes as
\[H_A^0(Z,P)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}H_B^0(Z,Q)\xrightarrow{b^*\otimes1}H_z^0(B,b^*P)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}H_B^0(Z,Q)\longrightarrow H_z^0(Z,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q),\]

where \(b^*\) is the restriction and the second arrow, a variant of the product considered in (SGA 41/2 Cycle 1.2.1), derives from the obvious product at the level of sections of sheaves (this assertion is verified easily, for example with the help of the flasque resolutions of (SGA 4 XVII 4.2); if \(a':\{z\}\longrightarrow B\), \(b':\{z\}\longrightarrow A\), \(c:\{z\}\longrightarrow Z\) are the inclusions, one can also invoke the standard decomposition of the Künneth arrow (III 4.2.1) \(a'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Zb'^!Q\longrightarrow c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\) into a "base change arrow" \(a'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Zb'^!Q\longrightarrow a'^!b^*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}a'^*b'^!Q\), followed by "projection arrows" \(a'^!b^*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}a'^*b'^!Q\longrightarrow a'^!(b^*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}b'^!Q)\longrightarrow a'^!b'^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\)). Taking \(P=DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM\), \(Q=L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SDM\), and going back to the definition of \(\langle u,v\rangle\) (III 4.2.5), we see that it suffices to prove that \(b^*u=0\), whence the conclusion.
Taking 2.4 into account, 1.2 will therefore follow from the following more precise result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5

Under the hypotheses of 1.2, we assume that \(a\) and \(b\) are closed immersions and that \(L_x=0\), \(M_y=0\). Denote by \(T\) the strict localization of \(X\times_S Y\) at \(z=(x,y)\), and identify \(A,B\) with closed subschemes of \(T\). Then the restriction arrows

\[b^*:R\Gamma_A(T,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M)|T)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_z(B,b^*(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M)),\] \[a^*:R\Gamma_B(T,(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DM)|T)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_z(A,a^*(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DM))\]

are zero.

2.6. The proof of 2.5 will occupy the rest of n° 2 and n°s 3 and 4. Taking into account the symmetry of the statement, we may restrict ourselves to proving the assertion relative to \(b^*\). We are going to re-express it in a form which will be more convenient for us. Note first that
\[R\Gamma(T,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M)|T)=(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M)_z=(DL)_x\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M_y=0,\]
since \(M_y=0\), and similarly that \(R\Gamma(B,b^*(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM))=(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)_z=0\). Consequently, the relative cohomology triangles give isomorphisms

\[R\Gamma(T-A,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|T-A)\xrightarrow{\sim}R\Gamma_A(T,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|T)[1],\] \[R\Gamma(B-z,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|B-z)\xrightarrow{\sim}R\Gamma_z(B,b^*(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM))[1],\]
by which the arrow \(b^*\) of 2.5, shifted by 1, is identified with the restriction
\[(2.6.1)\qquad b^*:R\Gamma(T-A,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|T-A)\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B-z,(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|B-z).\]
Since \(L_x=0\), \(M_y=0\), we can write
\[(2.6.2)\qquad L=i_!DP,\qquad M=j_!Q,\]

where \(i:X-x\longrightarrow X\), \(j:Y-y\longrightarrow Y\) denote the inclusions, and \(P\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X-x)\), \(Q\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y-y)\). By the duality isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.19.6) (plus a passage to the limit), we have \(DL=Ri_*DDP=Ri_*P\), hence \(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM=Ri_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Sj_!Q\).
Set \(X\times_SY=Z\), and identify \(X\times\{y\}\), \(\{x\}\times Y\) with closed subschemes of \(Z\) (resp. \(T\)), which we will denote simply \(X,Y\). Denote by
\[f':Z-Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow Z,\quad g':Z-(X\cup Y)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow Z-Y,\] \[f:T-Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T,\quad g:T-(X\cup Y)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T-Y,\]
the inclusions. The Künneth formula (III 1.6.4) gives, by passage to the limit, \(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM=Rf'_*g'_!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SQ)\), whence
\[(2.6.3)\qquad(DL\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)|T=Rf_*g_!((P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SQ)|T-(X\cup Y)).\]
With this identification, 2.6.1 reads
\[(2.6.4)\qquad b^*:R\Gamma(T-(Y\cup A),g_!(E)|T-(Y\cup A))\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B-z,g_!(E)|B-z),\]
where \(E=(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SQ)|T-(X\cup Y)\).

In the two sections which follow, we are going to show that the arrow 2.6.4 is zero: we will first reduce to the case where the cohomology sheaves of \(E\) have tame ramification (the action of the inertia taking place more precisely through a \(\ell\)-group, for a prime number \(\ell\ne p\)), then we will treat this case directly with the help of a purity theorem.
3. — Reduction to the tame case.

3.1. Recall that the base ring \(\Lambda\) is killed by an integer prime to \(p\), hence is a direct product of noetherian rings \(\Lambda_i\), each \(\Lambda_i\) being killed by a power of a prime number \(\ell_i\ne p\). For every scheme \(S'\) over \(S\), the derived category \(D(S',\Lambda)\) is the product of the categories \(D(S',\Lambda_i)\), so that, to prove the vanishing of 2.6.4, we may assume that \(\Lambda\) is killed by a power of a prime number \(\ell\ne p\). We may assume, on the other hand, that \(P\) (resp. \(Q\)) is bounded with locally free components of finite type.

3.2. Set \(X-x=U\), \(Y-y=V\). Let \(U''/U\) be a finite Galois covering with group \(G\) which trivializes the components of \(P\), \(H\) a \(\ell\)-Sylow subgroup of \(G\), \(U'=U''/H\) the quotient covering, \(X'\) the normalized strictly local trait of \(X\) in \(U'\) : \(X'/X\) is a finite, connected covering, of degree \(d_1\) prime to \(\ell\), and the representation of \(\pi_1(U')\) defined by the components of \(P'=P|U'\) factors through a finite \(\ell\)-group. Proceeding in the same way with \(Q\), we find a finite, connected covering \(Y'/Y\) of degree \(d_2\) prime to \(\ell\), inducing on \(V\) an étale covering \(V'\) such that the representation of \(\pi_1(V')\) defined by the components of \(Q'=Q|V'\) factors through a finite \(\ell\)-group. Let \(d=d_1e_1=d_2e_2=\operatorname{ppcm}(d_1,d_2)\). After possibly extracting a \(e_1\)-th (resp. \(e_2\)-th) root of a uniformizer of \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)), we may assume that \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)) has the same properties as before, with in addition \(d_1=d_2=d\).

Let \(x'\) (resp. \(y'\)) be the closed point of \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)), \(T'\) the strict localization of \(X'\times_SY'\) at \((x',y')\), \(z'\) the closed point of \(T'\), \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)) the inverse image of \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) in \(T'\),
\[E'=(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SQ)|T'-(X'\cup Y')=(P'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SQ')|T'-(X'\cup Y').\]
We then have a commutative square of restriction arrows

\[\xymatrix{R\Gamma(T-(Y\cup A),g_!(E)|T-(Y\cup A))\ar[r]^{b^*}\ar[d]&R\Gamma(B-z,g_!(E)|B-z)\ar[d]\\R\Gamma(T'-(Y'\cup A'),g'_!(E')|T'-(Y'\cup A'))\ar[r]^{b'^*}&R\Gamma(B'-z',g'_!(E')|B'-z')}\tag{1}\]

where \(b':B'\longrightarrow T'\), \(g':T'-(X'\cup Y')\longrightarrow T'-Y'\) are the inclusions. The covering \(T'/T\) is finite, of degree \(d^2\), and étale outside \(X\cup Y\), hence induces an étale covering of degree \(d^2\) of (B'-z') over (B-z). The composite of (1) and of the trace morphism
\[R\Gamma(B'-z',g'_!(E')|B'-z')\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B-z,g_!(E)|B-z)\]

is therefore multiplication by \(d^2\), which is an isomorphism, since \(\ell\) does not divide \(d\). To prove that the arrow 2.6.4 is zero, it therefore suffices to prove that \(b'^*=0\). We will thus be reduced to the case where the representation of \(\pi_1(U)\) (resp. \(\pi_1(V)\)) defined by the components of \(P\) (resp. \(Q\)) factors through a finite \(\ell\)-group, provided that we make sure that \(A',B'\) satisfy the general position hypotheses of 1.2. This will result from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

Under the hypotheses of 1.2, \(a\) and \(b\) being closed immersions, let \(X'/X\), \(Y'/Y\) be finite coverings of strictly local \(S\)-traits, of the same degree \(d\), and denote by \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)) the inverse image of \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) in \(X'\times_S Y'\). Then the pairs \((A',B')\), \((A',X')\), \((Y',B')\) are each in general position (in the sense of 1.2)(*).
The proof of 3.3 will be given after some preliminaries.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

Let \(X\xleftarrow{s_1}W\xrightarrow{s_2}Y\) be finite morphisms of \(S\)-traits, \(s=(s_1,s_2):W\longrightarrow X\times_S Y\), \(u,v,w\) uniformizers of \(X,Y,W\) respectively, so that

\[s_1^*u=aw^m\bmod w^{m+1},\qquad s_2^*v=bw^n\bmod w^{n+1},\]

with \(a,b\in k^*\). Then: (i) If \(m<n\), \(s(W)\) is tangent to \(X\) at \(z=(x,y)\).

(ii) If \(m=n\), \(s(W)\) is tangent to the subscheme of \(X\times_S Y\) with equation \(ap_2^*(v)-bp_1^*(u)=0\).

Let \(Z=X\times_S Y\), and \(W'\) the reduced closed subscheme image of \(s\). In the tangent space at \(z\) to \(Z\), the slope, with respect to the tangent to \(X\), of the tangent to \(W'\) is the value at the origin of the function induced on \(W'\) by \(p_2^*(v)/p_1^*(u)\) ; it is also the value at the origin of the function induced by \(p_2^*(v)/p_1^*(u)\) on \(W\), hence it is (0) (resp. \(b/a\)) in case (i) (resp. (ii)).

In the situation of 3.4, we will define the slope of \(W\) with respect to \(((X,u),(Y,v))\) as the element of \(k\cup\{\infty\}\) equal to (0) if \(m<n\), \(\infty\) if \(m>n\), \(b/a\) if \(m=n\). This element does not depend on the choice of \(w\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.5

Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\), \(g:Y'\longrightarrow Y\) be finite morphisms of \(S\)-traits with the same ramification index \(d\), \(u,v,u',v'\) uniformizers of \(X,Y,X',Y'\) respectively. Then there exists \(C\in k^*\) such that, for every commutative diagram of finite morphisms of \(S\)-traits

\[\xymatrix{X'&W'\ar[l]_{s'_1}\ar[r]^{s'_2}\ar[d]^h&Y'\\X&W\ar[l]^{s_1}\ar[r]_{s_2}&Y\ar[u]_g}\qquad\text{where the arrow }X'\to X\text{ is }f,\]

we have \(\lambda=C\lambda'^d\), where \(\lambda\) (resp. \(\lambda'\)) is the slope of \(W\) (resp. \(W'\)) with respect to \(((X,u),(Y,v))\) (resp. \(((X',u'),(Y',v'))\)).
By hypothesis, we have
\[f^*u=\alpha u'^d\bmod u'^{d+1},\qquad g^*v=\beta v'^d\bmod v'^{d+1},\]
with \(\alpha,\beta\in k^*\). Let us show that \(C=\beta/\alpha\) has the desired properties. Let \(w\) (resp. \(w'\)) be a uniformizer of \(W\) (resp. \(W'\)). We have
\[s_1^*u=aw^m\bmod w^{m+1},\qquad s_2^*v=bw^n\bmod w^{n+1},\] \[s_1'^*u'=a'w'^{m'}\bmod w'^{m'+1},\qquad s_2'^*v'=b'w'^{n'}\bmod w'^{n'+1},\] \[h^*w=\gamma w'^r\bmod w'^{r+1},\]
with \(a,b,a',b',\gamma\in k^*\). The commutativity of the squares of the diagram implies
\(m'd=rm\), \(n'd=rn\), whence \(m'/n'=m/n\). Suppose first \(m=n\). We have \(\lambda=b/a\), \(\lambda'=b'/a'\). If we set \(N=m'd=rm=n'd=rn\), the above congruences yield
\[(fs'_1)^*u=\alpha a'^d w'^N\bmod w'^{N+1},\qquad (s_1h)^*u=a\gamma^m w'^N\bmod w'^{N+1},\]

whence \(\alpha a'^d=a\gamma^m\). We obtain similarly \(\beta b'^d=b\gamma^m\), whence \(\lambda=C\lambda'^d\), for \(\lambda\ne0,\infty\). But, since \(m'/n'=m/n\), \(\lambda=0\) (resp. \(\infty\)) is equivalent to \(\lambda'=0\) (resp. \(\infty\)), which completes the proof.

Let us now prove 3.3. Let \(Z=X\times_S Y\), \(Z'=X'\times_S Y'\), \(\widetilde A\) (resp. \(\widetilde B\)) be the normalization of \(A\) (resp. \(B\)), \(\widetilde A'\) (resp. \(\widetilde B'\)) the normalization of \(A'\) (resp. \(B'\)). Since \(\widetilde A'\) (resp. \(\widetilde B'\)) is also the normalization of \(\widetilde A\times_Z Z'\) (resp. \(\widetilde B\times_Z Z'\)), we have commutative squares
\[\xymatrix{\widetilde A'\ar[r]\ar[d]&Z'\ar[d]&\widetilde B'\ar[l]\ar[d]\\\widetilde A\ar[r]&Z&\widetilde B\ar[l]}\tag{*}\]

Let \((x_i)_{1\leq i\leq m}\) (resp. \((y_j)_{1\leq j\leq n}\)) be the points of \(\widetilde A\) (resp. \(\widetilde B\)) above \(x\) (resp. \(y\)), \((x'_i)_{1\leq i\leq m'}\) (resp. \((y'_j)_{1\leq j\leq n'}\)) the points of \(\widetilde A'\) (resp. \(\widetilde B'\)) above \(x'\) (resp. \(y'\)). Since \(A\) and \(B\) are strictly local, we have decompositions \(\widetilde A=\coprod_{1\leq i\leq m}A_i\), \(\widetilde B=\coprod_{1\leq j\leq n}B_j\), \(\widetilde A'=\coprod_{1\leq i\leq m'}A'_i\), \(\widetilde B'=\coprod_{1\leq j\leq n'}B'_j\), where \(A_i,B_j,A'_i,B'_j\) are strictly local traits with respective closed points \(x_i,y_j,x'_i,y'_j\). Let us choose uniformizers \(u,v,u',v'\), of \(X,Y,X',Y'\) respectively, and denote by \(\lambda_i\) (resp. \(\mu_j\)) the slope of \(A_i\) (resp. \(B_j\)) with respect to \(((X,u),(Y,v))\), and by \(\lambda'_i\) (resp. \(\mu'_j\)) that of \(A'_i\) (resp. \(B'_j\)) with respect to \(((X',u'),(Y',v'))\). The general position hypotheses on \(A\) and \(B\) are expressed by
\[(1)\qquad \lambda_i e0\ ext{for all }i,\quad \mu_j e\infty\ ext{for all }j,\quad \lambda_i e\mu_j\ ext{for all }i,j,\]
and we must show that we have
\[(2)\qquad \lambda'_i\ne0\ \text{for all }i,\quad \mu'_j\ne\infty\ \text{for all }j,\quad \lambda'_i\ne\mu'_j\ \text{for all }i,j.\]
Let \(i'\in[1,m']\), \(j'\in[1,n']\), \(x_i\) (resp. \(y_j\)) the image of \(x'_{i'}\) (resp. \(y'_{j'}\)). The diagram (*) yields commutative squares of finite morphisms of \(S\)-traits
\[\xymatrix{X'&A'_{i'}\ar[l]\ar[r]\ar[d]&Y'\ar[d]\\X&A_i\ar[l]\ar[r]&Y}\qquad,\qquad \xymatrix{X'\ar[d]&B'_{j'}\ar[l]\ar[r]\ar[d]&Y'\\X&B_j\ar[l]\ar[r]&Y}.\]
By 3.5, there exists \(C\in k^*\) such that \(\lambda_i=C\lambda_{i'}'^d\), \(\mu_j=C\mu_{j'}'^d\), so (1) implies (2), which completes the proof of 3.3.

footnotes.html: with the usual abuse of notation \(X'=X'\times\{y'\}\), etc. (where \(x'\) (resp. \(y'\)) is the closed point of \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)).

blocks.html: 4. — End of the proof of 1.2.

4.1 To complete the proof of 2.5, hence of 1.2, it remains to establish the vanishing of 2.6.4 under the following additional hypotheses: \(\Lambda\) is killed by a power of a prime number \(\ell\ne p\), \(P\) and \(Q\) are bounded, with locally free components of finite type, such that the corresponding representations of the fundamental groups factor through finite \(\ell\)-groups. We have

where \(g_1:T-(X\cup Y\cup A)\longrightarrow T-(Y\cup A)\) is the inclusion, and \(E_1=(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S Q)|T-(X\cup Y\cup A)\). Now \(E_1\) is a complex with bounded, constructible, locally constant cohomology, such that the corresponding representation of \(\pi_1(T-(X\cup Y\cup A))\) factors through a \(\ell\)-group. The assertion to be proved will therefore result from the following more general statement:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let \(T\) be the strict localization at a closed point of a smooth \(S\)-scheme of dimension 2, \(t\) the closed point of \(T\), \(A,B,X\) closed subschemes of dimension 1 of \(T\), \(i:T-(A\cup X)\longrightarrow T-A\) the inclusion. We assume that \(X\) is regular, and that \(A\) is in general position with respect to \(B\) and \(X\) (i.e. that the intersection of the tangent cones at \(t\) to \(A\) and \(B\cup X\) is reduced to \(t\)). Let on the other hand \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(T-(A\cup X))\), such that \(H^*(E)\) is constructible, locally constant, and such that the corresponding representation of \(\pi_1(T-(A\cup X))\) factors through a \(\ell\)-group. Then the restriction arrow

\[R\Gamma(T-A,i_!(E))\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B-t,i_!(E)|B-t)\]

is zero.
The proof will rely on the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3

Let \(Z\) be a regular connected noetherian scheme of dimension 2, with residue characteristics prime to \(\ell\), \(X,Y\) regular divisors meeting transversally at a point \(z\), whence a diagram of inclusions

\[ \xymatrix{ Z-(X\cup Y) \ar[r]^{i'} \ar[d]_{j'} & Z-Y \ar[d]^j\\ Z-X \ar[r]^i & Z\\ Y-z \ar[u]^{m'} \ar[r]_{i_1} & Y \ar[u]_m } \]

We make the purity hypothesis:

\[ \tag{P} R^qj_*(\mathbb Z/\ell)=0\qquad\text{for }q>1. \]

Let on the other hand \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(Z-(X\cup Y),\Lambda)\), with constructible locally constant cohomology, such that the corresponding representation of \(\pi_1(Z-(X\cup Y))\) factors through a \(\ell\)-group. Then:

a) We have a canonical isomorphism

\[ i_1^*m'^*Rj'_*L\overset\sim\longrightarrow m^*Rj_*(i'_!L), \]

and the complex \(m'^*Rj'_*L\) has constructible, locally constant cohomology.

b) Suppose \(Y\) connected, and let \(\bar s\) be a geometric point of \(Y\) localized at a closed point \(s\ne z\). Then the restriction arrow

\[ \tag{4.3.1}R\Gamma(Y,m^*Rj_*(i'_!L))\longrightarrow (Rj_*(i'_!L))_{\bar s} \]

is zero.

c) Under the hypotheses of b), let \(C\) be a closed subscheme of dimension 1 of \(Z\) such that \(Y\cap C=s\), and denote by \(\widetilde C\) the strict localization of \(C\) at \(\bar s\). Then the restriction arrow

\[ \tag{4.3.2}R\Gamma(Z-Y,i'_!L)\longrightarrow R\Gamma(\widetilde C-\bar s,i'_!(L)|\widetilde C-\bar s) \]

is zero.
The (trivial) isomorphism \(i_1^*Rj_*i'_!L\overset\sim\longrightarrow Rj'_*(i'^*i'_!L)=Rj'_*L\) gives an
isomorphism \(m'^*Rj'_*L\overset\sim\longrightarrow i_1^*m^*Rj_*i'_!L\), whence, by adjunction, an arrow
\[ i_{1!}m'^*Rj'_*L\longrightarrow m^*Rj_*(i'_!L), \]
which induces an isomorphism outside \(z\). Let us show that it is an isomorphism, i.e. that, if \(\bar z\) is a geometric point above \(z\),

For this we may replace \(\Lambda\) by \(\mathbb Z/\ell^ u\), where \(\ell^ u\) kills \(\Lambda\), and we reduce, by dévissage and passage to the limit, to the case where \(L\) is a locally constant \(\mathbb Z/\ell^ u\)-Module of finite type, corresponding to a representation of the \(\pi_1\) through a \(\ell\)-group. Since the only finite abelian group of \(\ell\)-torsion which is simple under the action of a \(\ell\)-group is \(\mathbb Z/\ell\) with trivial action, we reduce, by a further dévissage, to the case where \(L\) is the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z/\ell\). Let \(\widetilde Z\) be the strict localization of \(Z\) at \( ar z\), \(\widetilde X=X imes_Z\widetilde Z\), \(\widetilde Y=Y imes_Z\widetilde Z\), \(i':\widetilde Z-(\widetilde X\cup\widetilde Y)\longrightarrow\widetilde Z-\widetilde Y\) the inclusion. We have
\[ Rj'_*(i'_!(\mathbb Z/\ell))_{\bar z}=R\Gamma(\widetilde Z-\widetilde Y,i'_!(\mathbb Z/\ell)), \]
so that we are reduced to proving that the restriction arrow
\[ H^n(\widetilde Z-\widetilde Y,\mathbb Z/\ell)\longrightarrow H^n(\widetilde X-z,\mathbb Z/\ell) \]

is an isomorphism for every \(n\). Now, for \(n\leq 1\), this is a consequence of Abhyankar's lemma, and for \(n>1\) both sides are zero by the purity hypothesis. The first assertion of a) is thus established. Let us prove the second. By dévissage, we may assume \(L\) concentrated in degree 0. The hypothesis \(P\) implies, by dévissage, \(R^qj'_*L=0\) for \(q>2\). It remains to prove the constructibility and local constancy of \(m'^*R^qj'_*L\) for \(q\leq1\), which is standard. For the constructibility, resolving \(L\) on the right by sheaves of the type \(p_*p^*L\), where \(p:Z'\longrightarrow Z\) is a finite covering tamely ramified along \(Y\), we reduce to \(L\) constant, with value \(L_0\) : we then have \(m'^*j'_*L=(L_0)_{Y-z}\), and \(m'^*R^1j'_*L=(L_0)_{Y-z}(-1)\) by Abhyankar. For the local constancy, we must see that the specialization arrows are isomorphisms. For this, we may,

as above, replace \(\Lambda\) by \(\mathbb Z/\ell^\nu\), and we reduce by dévissage to the case where \(L\) is the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z/\ell\) : we conclude with the help of Abhyankar's lemma. We have thus proved a). Let us prove b). Let \(\bar\eta\) be a geometric generic point of \(Y\). Choose specialization arrows \(a:\bar\eta\longrightarrow Y(\bar z)\), \(b:\bar\eta\longrightarrow Y(\bar s)\) (where \(Y(\bar z)\) (resp. \(Y(\bar s)\)) is the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(\bar z\) (resp. \(\bar s\))). Set \(M=m^*Rj_*(i'_!L)\). The commutative square
\[ \xymatrix{ Y & Y(\bar s)\ar[l]\\ Y(\bar z)\ar[u] & \bar\eta\ar[l]_a\ar[u]^b } \]
induces a commutative square
\[ \xymatrix{ R\Gamma(Y,M)\ar[r]^{4.3.1}\ar[d] & M_{\bar s}\ar[d]^{b^*}\\ M_{\bar z}\ar[r]_{a^*} & M_{\bar\eta}. } \]
Since \(M|Y-z\) has locally constant cohomology, \(b^*\) is an isomorphism. On the other hand, since \(M=i_{1!}i_1^*M\), we have \(M_{\bar z}=0\), so the arrow 4.3.1 is zero.

It remains to prove c). We have \(\widetilde C=C\times_Z\widetilde Z\), where \(\widetilde Z\) denotes, as above, the strict localization of \(Z\) at \(\bar z\). Set \(\widetilde Y=Y\times_Z\widetilde Z\). We then have a commutative diagram of restriction arrows

\[ \xymatrix{ R\Gamma(Z-Y,i'_!L)=R\Gamma(Z,Rj_*(i'_!L))\ar[r]^{4.3.2}\ar[d] & R\Gamma(\widetilde C-\bar s,i'_!(L)|\widetilde C-\bar s)\\ R\Gamma(Y,m^*Rj_*(i'_!L))\ar[r]^{4.3.1} & (Rj_*(i'_!L))_{\bar s}=R\Gamma(\widetilde Z-\widetilde Y,(i'_!L)|\widetilde Z-\widetilde Y)\ar[u], } \]
the vanishing of 4.3.2 therefore follows from b), which completes the proof of 4.3.

Let us prove 4.2. Let \(f:T'\longrightarrow T\) be the blow-up of the maximal ideal of \(T\), \(D=f^{-1}(z)\;(\sim\mathbb P^1_S)\) the exceptional divisor, \(A',B',X'\) the respective strict transforms of \(A,B,X\). Thus \(T'\) is regular, connected, of dimension 2, \(X'\) is a regular divisor meeting \(D\) transversally at a point \(z'\), and the general position hypotheses on \(A,B,X\) mean that \(A'\) is disjoint from

\(B'\cup X'\). Denote by \(E'\) the inverse image of \(E\) on \(T'-(A'\cup D\cup X')\) and by \(i':T'-(A'\cup D\cup X')\longrightarrow T'-(A'\cup D)\) the inclusion. We must prove that the restriction arrow
\[ \tag{*}R\Gamma(T'-(A'\cup D),i'_!(E'))\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B'\cup D,i'_!(E')|B'-(B'\cup D)) \]

is zero. But \(B'\) is a disjoint union of strictly local schemes \(B'_j\;(1\leq j\leq n)\), of dimension 1, such that, for each \(j\), \(B'_j\cap D\) is reduced to a point \(b_j\). Applying 4.3 c) to \((Z=T'-A',\ X=X',\ Y=D-(D\cap A'),\ L=E',\ C=B'_j)\) (the hypothesis \(P\) is satisfied by virtue of the relative purity theorem (SGA 4 XVI 3), for \((Z,Y)\) is a filtered projective limit of smooth pairs of codimension 1 over \(S\), with affine étale transition morphisms), we find that the restriction arrow
\[ R\Gamma(T'-(A'\cup D),i'_!(E'))\longrightarrow R\Gamma(B'_j-b_j,i'_!(E')|B'_j-b_j) \]
is zero. Hence (*) is zero, which completes the proof of 4.2, hence of 2.5 and 1.2.
II. Equivariant correspondences.
5. — Non-commutative traces.

In this section we fix a topos \(T\) and a unitary commutative ring \(K\) of \(T\). The rings considered will be assumed associative and unitary, but not necessarily commutative. We will call a \(K\)-algebra any (associative, unitary) ring \(A\) of \(T\) equipped with a (unitary) homomorphism \(K\longrightarrow A\) whose image is contained in the center.
5.0. — Introduction.
Let \(E\) be a \(K\)-module which is locally a direct factor of a free module of finite type. We then have a trace homomorphism (of local nature)
\[ \tag{5.0.1}\operatorname{Tr}_K:\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,E)\longrightarrow K \]
defined by composition of the "evaluation arrow"
\[ \tag{5.0.2}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,K)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow K, \qquad f\otimes x\longmapsto f(x), \]
with the inverse of the canonical "tensor product" isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.0.3}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,K)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,E),\qquad f\otimes y\longmapsto(x\longmapsto f(x)y). \]
The arrows 5.0.2 and 5.0.3 are defined more generally for every \(K\)-module \(E\), and from them we deduce, for every complex of \(K\)-modules \(E\), homomorphisms of complexes

\[ \tag{5.0.4}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K^\bullet(E,K)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow K, \qquad f\otimes x\longmapsto f(x), \] \[ \tag{5.0.5}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K^\bullet(E,K)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K^\bullet(E,E),\qquad f\otimes y\longmapsto(x\longmapsto f(x)y). \]
By derivation, 5.0.4, 5.0.5 give, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(K)\), arrows of \(D(K)\) :

\[ \tag{5.0.6}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,K)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE\longrightarrow K, \] \[ \tag{5.0.7}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,K)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,E). \]

If \(E\) is perfect (SGA 6 I), 5.0.7 is an isomorphism (as one verifies easily by reduction to the case where \(E=K\)), and, by composing the inverse of 5.0.7 with 5.0.6, we obtain a trace homomorphism, of global nature,
\[ \tag{5.0.8}\operatorname{Tr}_K:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,E)\longrightarrow K, \]
which generalizes 5.0.1 (for more details, see (SGA 6 I 7, 8)).

We propose here to examine to what extent one can, in the preceding constructions, replace \(K\) by a \(K\)-algebra \(A\) (we will be interested mainly in the case where \(A\) is the \(K\)-algebra of an ordinary finite group), and in particular to define a trace arrow analogous to 5.0.8, generalizing the notion of "non-commutative" trace introduced by Stallings in [5] and developed by Grothendieck in exposé XI of this seminar (as was said in the introduction, this exposé, written up by I. Bucur, has unfortunately been lost; the reader may however consult the exposé of Deligne (SGA 41/2 Rapport), where the essential points are indicated). While replacing \(K\) by \(A\) in 5.0.3 presents no difficulty, the same is not true in 5.0.2: if \(E\) is a left \(A\)-module, the evaluation arrow
\[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow A, \qquad f\otimes x\longmapsto f(x) \]
does not factor in general through \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A)\otimes_AE\), but gives, by passage to the quotient, an arrow
\[ \tag{5.0.9}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A)\otimes_AE\longrightarrow A_\natural, \]

where \(A_\natural\) is the \(K\)-module quotient of \(A\) by the sub-\(K\)-module generated locally by the local sections of the form (ab-ba). If \(E\) is locally a direct factor of a free left \(A\)-module of finite type, the tensor product arrow, analogous to 5.0.3,
\[ \tag{5.0.10}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A)\otimes_AE\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E) \]
is an isomorphism, and we obtain, by composing the inverse of 5.0.10 with 5.0.9, a trace homomorphism
\[ \tag{5.0.11}\operatorname{Tr}_A:\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\longrightarrow A_\natural, \]

which reduces to 5.0.1 when \(A=K\). One verifies easily, moreover (see 5.7), that, for \(T\) punctual, 5.0.11 coincides with the trace homomorphism of Stallings and Grothendieck. In this section, we will examine the extension to complexes and the derivation of the arrows 5.0.9, 5.0.10, 5.0.11, and the behavior of the arrows obtained with respect to extension and restriction of scalars and to certain external tensor products. In fact, we will derive arrows a little more general than 5.0.9 and 5.0.10, so that the constructions can be applied to the definition, in n° 6, of "non-commutative" Lefschetz-Verdier local terms.
5.1. — Notations.

If \(A\) is a \(K\)-algebra, we shall denote by \(A^o\) the algebra opposite to \(A\), and by \(A^e=A\otimes_KA^o\) the enveloping algebra of \(A\). It amounts to the same thing to give a \(A\)-bimodule, a left \(A^e\)-module, or a right \(A^e\)-module. In particular, \(A\) is an \(A\)-bimodule, hence a left \(A^e\)-module and a right \(A^e\)-module. If \(E\) is a bimodule, recall ([1] IX 4) that
\[ H_o(A,E)=A\otimes_{A^e}E=E\otimes_{A^e}A \]

is the quotient \(K\)-module of \(E\) by the sub-\(K\)-module generated locally by the local sections of the form (ax-xa), for \(x\in E, a\in A\)(1). We shall sometimes write \(E_\natural\) instead of \(H_o(A,E)\). In particular, \(A_\natural\) is the \(K\)-module defined in 5.0.9. If \(A=K[G]\), where \(G\) is an ordinary finite group, \(A_\natural\) is none other than the free \(K\)-module on the set \(G_\natural\) of conjugacy classes of \(G\).

If \(A,B\) are \(K\)-algebras, we shall use the Cartan-Eilenberg notation \({}_A E, E_B, {}_A E_B\) to denote respectively a left \(A\)-module, a right \(B\)-module, a module which is a left module over \(A\) and a right module over \(B\). Finally, we shall denote by \(D(A)\) (resp. \(D(A,B)\)) the derived category of the category of left \(A\)-modules (resp. of \(A\)-\(B\)-bimodules, left over \(A\), right over \(B\).
5.2. Let \(A,B\) be \(K\)-algebras. Recall that, in the situation \(({}_A E,{}_B F_A,{}_B G)\), we have a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{5.2.1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F\otimes_AE,G), \quad f\longmapsto(y\otimes x\longmapsto f(x)(y)). \]
This derives to an isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.2.2}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE,G) \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B,A)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B)\). Indeed we may assume \(E\) bounded above and with flat components, \(G\) bounded below and with injective components; we then have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE,G)= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F\otimes_AE,G)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F,G)\), and \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F,G))= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F,G))\) since, if \(E\) is acyclic, \(F\otimes_AE\) is acyclic, hence \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F,G)) \simeq\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F\otimes_AE,G)\) is acyclic; for \(E\) and \(G\) as above, 5.2.2 is given by the isomorphism of complexes
\[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F,G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B^\bullet(F\otimes_AE,G) \]

defined by 5.2.1. Note that, if \(A\) and \(B\) are flat over \(K\), 5.2.2 still holds under the conditions \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B,A)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B)\), as one sees by resolving \(F\) by flat \((B\otimes_KA^o)\)-modules. From 5.2.2 we deduce an isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.2.3}\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_B(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE,G). \]
5.3. Let \(A\) be a \(K\)-algebra. In the situation \((E_A,{}_A F)\), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.3.1}E\otimes_AF\xrightarrow{\sim}(E\otimes_KF)\otimes_{A^e}A, \qquad x\otimes y\longmapsto(x\otimes y)\otimes1. \]
On the other hand, in the situation \(({}_A E,{}_A F_A)\), the evaluation arrow
\[ \tag{5.3.2}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\otimes_KE\longrightarrow F, \qquad f\otimes x\longmapsto f(x) \]
is \(A^e\)-linear, hence induces, via 5.3.1, by application of \(-\otimes_{A^e}A\), an arrow
\[ \tag{5.3.3}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\otimes_AE\longrightarrow F\otimes_{A^e}A\;(=F_\natural). \]
In other words, the arrow composed of 5.3.2 and the canonical arrow \(F\longrightarrow F_\natural, x\longmapsto x\otimes1\), factors (in a unique way) through 5.3.3.
Suppose \(A\) flat over \(K\). Then 5.3.1 derives to an isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.3.4}E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF\xrightarrow{\sim} (E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A, \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A^o)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\) (resolve \(E\) and \(F\) by flat modules). On the other hand, 5.3.3 derives to an arrow of \(D(K)\), called the evaluation arrow,
\[ \tag{5.3.5}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE \longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A, \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\) such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^o)\) : we first derive 5.3.2 to an arrow of \(D^-( (A^e)^o)\)
\[ \tag{5.3.6}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE \longrightarrow F, \]
by resolving \(E\) by flat modules and \(F\) by injective \(A^e\)-modules, then we apply the functor \(-\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\) to 5.3.6, taking account of 5.3.4.
5.4. Let \(A\) be a \(K\)-algebra. In the situation \(({}_A E,{}_A F_A,{}_A G)\), we have a canonical arrow
\[ \tag{5.4.1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\otimes_AG\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\otimes_AG),\qquad u\otimes y\longmapsto(x\longmapsto u(x)\otimes y), \]
which corresponds by adjunction (5.2.1) to the \(A\)-linear arrow
\[ E\otimes_K\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\otimes_AG\longrightarrow F\otimes_AG \]
defined by the evaluation arrow \(E\otimes_K\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\longrightarrow F\).
The arrow 5.4.1 derives to an arrow of \(D(K)\), called the tensor product arrow,
\[ \tag{5.4.2}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG), \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A^e)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), with \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A)\). If \(E\) and \(G\) are bounded above and with flat components, \(F\) bounded below and with injective components, the first member of 5.4.2 indeed identifies with \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,F)\otimes_AG\), and we send it into the second member by the composite of 5.4.1 and the natural arrow
\[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,F\otimes_AG)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\otimes_AG)= R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG). \]

The arrow 5.4.2 is not in general an isomorphism. It is one, however, in certain important cases, for example if \(E\) is perfect (as one checks by reducing to \(E=A\)). We shall see further on another interesting example (6.2.3).

The arrows 5.3.5 and 5.4.2 will play an essential role in all that follows in this exposé. The examples of objects \(F\) that we have in view are: a) \(F=A\), or \(F=A\) considered as a right \(A\)-module in the natural way and as a left \(A\)-module via a homomorphism of \(K\)-algebras \(\varphi:A\longrightarrow A\) (cf. 5.13); b) \(F=M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KA\), with \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(K)\) (in no. 6, \(M\) will be a dualizing complex, see in particular 6.3.2, 6.4.1, 6.5.6).

5.5. Let us denote by \(DF(A)\) the derived category of complexes of left \(A\)-modules equipped with a finite filtration ([3] V). Then, in the situation of 5.2, we have a canonical isomorphism of \(DF(K)\), analogous to 5.2.2, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-F(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-F(B,A)\;(=D^-F(B\otimes_KA^o))\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+F(B)\). Similarly, we have "filtered derived" arrows analogous to 5.3.4, 5.3.5, 5.3.6, 5.4.2. These arrows are compatible with passage to the associated graded objects, in the sense of ([3] V 1,2).

5.6. We assume \(A\) flat over \(K\). Let us denote by \(D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) the full subcategory of \(D(A)\) formed of the complexes of finite perfect amplitude, i.e. perfect and of finite tor-dimension (SGA 6 I). We define a canonical arrow, called the trace,
\[ \tag{5.6.1}\operatorname{Tr}_A=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A, \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\), by composing the inverse isomorphism of 5.4.2 for \(G=E\) with the evaluation arrow 5.3.5. From this we deduce a trace arrow
\[ \tag{5.6.2}\operatorname{Tr}_A:\operatorname{Hom}_A (E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)\longrightarrow H^0(T,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A). \]
For \(F=A\), composing with the canonical arrow \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\longrightarrow A\otimes_{A^e}A=A_\natural\) (5.1), we obtain arrows, again called traces,
\[ \tag{5.6.3}\operatorname{Tr}_A:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\longrightarrow A_\natural, \] \[ \tag{5.6.4}\operatorname{Tr}_A:\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,E)\longrightarrow H^0(T,A_\natural). \]
Note that 5.6.4 is a rather innocuous arrow, of local nature, while 5.6.3 is a "serious" arrow, of global nature.

Let us denote by \(DF(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) the full subcategory of \(DF(A)\) formed of the filtered complexes whose graded object is of finite perfect amplitude. Then we have an arrow of \(DF(K)\) analogous to 5.6.1 for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}DF(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^bF(A^e)\), whence arrows analogous to 5.6.2, 5.6.3, 5.6.4. It follows from the remarks of 5.5 that the filtered arrow 5.6.1 is compatible with passage to the associated graded object (cf. ([3] V 3.7.2)), and we deduce from this, as in (loc. cit.), that, if \(F\) is of filtration concentrated in degree 0, we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{DF(A)}(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)\),
\[ \tag{5.6.5}\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\sum_i\operatorname{Tr}_A\operatorname{gr}^i u. \]
This formula implies in particular that, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)\), we have
\[ \tag{5.6.6}\operatorname{Tr}(u[1])=-\operatorname{Tr}(u), \]
where \(u[1]:E[1]\longrightarrow(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)[1]\) is the translate of \(u\).
5.7. The traces defined in 5.6 generalize at once the "usual" commutative traces \((SGA 6 I), ([3] V 3)\), and the "noncommutative" traces of Stallings [5] and Grothendieck.
a) Suppose \(A=K\). Then \(A^e=A_\natural=K\), the arrow 5.6.1 is written
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_K:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K (E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE)\longrightarrow F \]

and coincides with the trace arrow defined in ([3] V 3.7.1) (in the particular case where the complex \(K\) of (loc. cit.) is reduced to \(A\)). In particular, \(\operatorname{Tr}_K:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(E,E)\longrightarrow K\) coincides with the trace defined in (SGA 6 I 8).

b) We assume again that \(A\) is a flat \(K\)-algebra (not necessarily commutative). We propose to make 5.6.4 explicit for \(E\) strictly perfect (i.e. bounded, with components locally direct factors of free modules of finite type). First of all, take \(E=A\) ; \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(A,A)= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(A,A)\) identifies with \(A\) by \(f\longmapsto f(1)\), \(a\longmapsto(x\longmapsto xa)\), and 5.6.3 identifies with the canonical projection \(A\longrightarrow A_\natural\), which we shall denote \(a\longmapsto a_\natural\), hence if \(d_a\) denotes right multiplication by \(a\),
\[ \tag{5.7.1}\operatorname{Tr}_A(d_a)=a_\natural. \]
Let, for \(1\leq i\leq n\), \(E_i\) be a copy of \(A\), and \(E=\bigoplus_{1\leq i\leq n}E_i\). The isomorphism 5.4.2 for \(F=A\), \(G=E\) is written
\[ \bigoplus_{1\leq i,j\leq n}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E_i,A)\otimes_AE_j \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E), \]
and, for \(u=\sum u_{ij}\otimes1\in\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\) (\(u_{ij}\otimes1\in\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E_i,A)\otimes_AE_j\)), we have
\[ \tag{5.7.2}\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\sum_i u_{ii\natural}, \]

by definition of the evaluation arrow 5.3.5. Let us now take for \(E\) a direct factor of \(A^n\), image of a projector \(p\), and denote by \(i:E\longrightarrow A^n\) the inclusion; then, for \(u\in\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\), we have
\[ \tag{5.7.3}\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(iup), \]

as one sees either with the help of 5.6.5, or directly from the definition. Finally, suppose \(E\) strictly perfect; if \(u\in\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\) is a homomorphism of complexes, we have, by 5.6.5, 5.6.6,
\[ \tag{5.7.4}\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}_A(u^i). \]

The fact that 5.6.4 comes, by application of \(H^0\), from a homomorphism of complexes \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A^\bullet(E,E)\longrightarrow A_\natural\) (5.6.3) implies that, if \(u,v\in\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E)\) are homotopic, we have
\[ \tag{5.7.5}\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(v). \]

Since every endomorphism of \(E\) in \(D(A)\) is locally defined by an endomorphism of complexes, locally unique up to homotopy (SGA 6 I 4.10), the formulas 5.7.2 to 5.7.4 thus permit the calculation of \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)\) for every endomorphism \(u\) of \(E\) in \(D(A)\), and, combined with 5.7.5, furnish a direct definition of \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)\). In the case where the topos \(T\) is punctual, we recover the definition of (SGA 41/2 Rapport 4.3).
5.8. Let \(P,Q\) be \(A\)-bimodules. Then \(P\otimes_AQ\) and \(Q\otimes_AP\) are \(A\)-bimodules, and we have canonical isomorphisms
\[ \tag{5.8.1}(P\otimes_AQ)\otimes_{A^e}A\xrightarrow{\sim}P\otimes_{A^e}Q \xleftarrow{\sim}(Q\otimes_AP)\otimes_{A^e}A\xleftarrow{\sim} A\otimes_{A^e}(P\otimes_KQ)\otimes_{A^e}A, \]

each of the objects above identifying canonically with the quotient \(K\)-module of \(P\otimes_KQ\) by the submodule generated by the sections of the form \(axb\otimes y-x\otimes bya\), for \(x\in P\), \(y\in Q\), \(a,b\in K\). For \(A\) flat over \(K\), these isomorphisms derive to isomorphisms

\[ \tag{5.8.2}(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AQ)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \simeq P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}Q\simeq (Q\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AP)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \simeq A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e} (P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KQ)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \]
for \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A^e)\). On the other hand, the composition arrow at the level of modules,

\[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P\otimes_AF)\otimes_K \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(F,Q\otimes_AE)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P\otimes_AQ\otimes_AE), \quad f\otimes g\longmapsto(P\otimes_Ag)f, \]
derives to

\[ \tag{5.8.3}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(F,Q\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AQ\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \]

for \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\), \(E,F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A)\) such that the tensor products and \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) appearing above are in \(D^b\). When \(E\) and \(F\) are perfect, this arrow identifies, via 5.4.2, with the arrow deduced from the evaluation arrow \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(F,Q)\longrightarrow Q\) ; from this one easily deduces that we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.8.4} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (F,Q\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[r]^{(1)} \ar[d]_{(2)} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AQ\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A} \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (F,Q\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AP\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_A} & P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}Q, } \]

where (1) and (2) are given by 5.8.3 (the point is that the two composite arrows of 5.8.4 are defined by the tensor product of the evaluation arrows \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(F,Q)\longrightarrow Q\), \(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P)\longrightarrow P\)). It follows that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(F,Q\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)\), we have
\[ \tag{5.8.5}\operatorname{Tr}_A(vu)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(uv). \]
We shall sometimes denote by \(\langle u,v\rangle_A\) the common value of 5.8.5.
5.9. Traces and extension of scalars.

Let \(A\longrightarrow B\) be a morphism of flat \(K\)-algebras. From it we deduce a morphism of \(K\)-algebras \(A^e\longrightarrow B^e\), whence an extension of scalars functor \(-\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e:D(A^e)\longrightarrow D(B^e)\). For \(E,F\) as in 5.3.5, if \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^e)\), and \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^o)\), we have a commutative square of \(D(K)\)

\[ \tag{5.9.1} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE \ar[r]^{(1)} \ar[d]_{(3)} & F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \ar[d]^{(4)} \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[r]_{(2)} & (F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B, } \]

where the arrows (1) and (2) are defined by 5.3.6, (4) by \(A\longrightarrow B\), and (3) by the canonical arrow \(F\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\). The verification is trivial. Similarly, for \(E,F,G\) as in 5.4.2, if \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B^e)\), \((F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG \in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B)\), we
have a commutative square of \(D(K)\)

\[ \tag{5.9.2} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG \ar[r]^{(1)} \ar[d]_{(3)} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG) \ar[d]^{(4)} \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG) \ar[r]_{(2)} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, (F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG)). } \]

where (1) and (2) are defined by 5.4.2, (3) and (4) by \(F\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\). From this we deduce, for \(E,F\) as in 5.6.1, with \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^e)\), a commutative square of \(D(K)\), analogous to the preceding ones, where the vertical arrows are defined by 5.6.1

\[ \tag{5.9.3} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A} \ar[d] & F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \ar[d] \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, (F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_B} & (F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B. } \]

Finally, if we are given \(F'\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^e)\) such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F')\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^o)\), and \(f:F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\longrightarrow F'\), we have a compatibility slightly more general than 5.9.1, which is expressed by an analogous commutative square, with \(F'\) instead of \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\) in the lower line, and the vertical arrows defined by \(f\). We likewise have commutative squares analogous to 5.9.2 and 5.9.3 with \(F'\) instead of \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\). Taking in particular \(F=A\) and for \(f:A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}B^e\longrightarrow B\) the arrow defined by \(A\longrightarrow B\), we obtain a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.9.4} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,E) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A} \ar[d] & A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \ar[d] \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE, B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_B} & B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B, } \]
where the left (resp. right) vertical arrow is given by extension of scalars (resp. is the canonical arrow deduced from \(A\longrightarrow B\)).
5.10. Traces and restriction of scalars.

Let \(A\longrightarrow B\) be a morphism of flat \(K\)-algebras such that \(B\), as a left \(A\)-module, is flat and of finite presentation (i.e. locally a direct factor of a free \(A\)-module of finite type). This is the case for example if \(A\longrightarrow B\) is the morphism \(K[H]\longrightarrow K[G]\) defined by an injective morphism of discrete groups \(H\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow G\), of finite index. The restriction of scalars homomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B,B)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,B)\), where \(B\) is considered as a left module, identifies, by the canonical isomorphisms \(B\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B,B)\), \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_AB \xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,B)\), with an arrow (of \(B\)-bimodules)
\[ \tag{5.10.1}B\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_AB. \]

The composite of 5.10.1 with the evaluation arrow \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_AB\longrightarrow A_\natural\) (5.3.3) is the homomorphism \(f\longmapsto\operatorname{Tr}_A(f)\) (\(\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=\operatorname{Tr}_A(d_f)\), where \(d_f\) is right multiplication by \(f\)), hence by the formula \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(fg)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(gf)\) (5.8.5), factors through \(B_\natural\) into an arrow again denoted \(\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}:B_\natural\longrightarrow A_\natural\) : we thus have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.10.2} \xymatrix{ B \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B} \ar[d]_{5.10.1} & B_\natural \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}} \\ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_AB \ar[r]_{\mathrm{ev}} & A_\natural. } \]
In the situation \(({}_BE,{}_BP_A)\), we have a homomorphism of right \(B\)-modules
\[ \tag{5.10.3} \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,P)\otimes_AB\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,P\otimes_AB),\quad u\otimes b\longmapsto(x\mapsto u(x)\otimes b), \]

which is an isomorphism, as one checks immediately (it suffices to see that the underlying \(K\)-linear homomorphism is an isomorphism, and this follows from the fact that \(B\) is locally a direct factor of a free \(A\)-module of finite type). This isomorphism derives to an isomorphism of \(D(B)\)
\[ \tag{5.10.4} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,P)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AB \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AB), \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B)\), \(P\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B\otimes_KA^o)\) (resolve \(E\) by modules of the form \(B\otimes_AL\), where \(L\) is flat, and \(P\) by injective \((B\otimes_KA^o)\)-modules, which are necessarily injective as \(B\)-modules, \(A\) being flat over \(K\) : this allows us "to remove the \(R\)" on the left, and the resolution taken for \(E\) also allows us to remove it on the right).
For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(K)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B)\), we define an arrow of \(D(X)\)(*)

\[ \tag{5.10.5} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF, \]
as the composite of the arrows

\[ \begin{aligned} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF &\xrightarrow{(1)}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B (E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AB \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF\\ &\xrightarrow{(2)}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B (E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF\\ &\xrightarrow{(3)}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B (E,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF\\ &\xrightarrow{(4)}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF, \end{aligned} \]

where (1) is defined by 5.10.1, (2) by the inverse isomorphism of 5.10.4 with \(P=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM\), (3) by the canonical isomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM \xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (B,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)\) (5.4.2), and (4) by the adjunction isomorphism 5.2.2.
Proposition 5.10.6 — Suppose that \(M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(B)\). We then have a commutative square of \(D(K)\)

\[ \tag{5.10.7} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF \ar[r]^{5.4.2} \ar[d]_{5.10.5} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF) \ar[d] \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF \ar[r]_{5.4.2} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF), } \]
where the right vertical arrow is the restriction of scalars.
It is a matter of checking that we have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF \ar[r] \ar[d]_{E\otimes 5.10.5} & M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF \ar[d]^{\text{restriction of scalars}} \\ E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AF \ar[r] & M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF, } \]

where the horizontal arrows are given by the evaluations (5.3.6). This square is deduced by application of \(-\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BF\) from the analogous square with \(F=B\), hence we may assume \(F=B\). The commutativity of 5.10.7 in this case is equivalent to that of the triangle

\[ \tag{*} \xymatrix{ &R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \ar[dl]_{(1)} \ar[dr]^{(2)}&\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E, \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)) \ar[rr]_{(3)}&& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM), } \]

where (1) is defined by 5.10.1, (2) is the restriction of scalars, and (3) the adjunction isomorphism. Now the arrow \(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM)\) given by 5.10.1 is none other than the adjunction arrow corresponding to the restriction of scalars functor and \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,-)\) ; the commutativity of (*) follows at once.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\) be such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^o)\). We then have, by 5.3.5, an evaluation arrow
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BE\longrightarrow M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B), \]
which gives, by composition with the canonical arrow \(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B\longrightarrow B_\natural\), an arrow
\[ \tag{5.10.8} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BE\longrightarrow M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KB_\natural. \]

Proposition 5.10.9 — Suppose \(A\) of finite presentation and flat as a \(K\)-module, and \(A_\natural\), \(B_\natural\) flat over \(K\). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(B)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\) be such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \in\operatorname{ob}D^b(B^o)\). Then \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^o)\), and we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.10.10} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BE \ar[r]^{5.10.8} \ar[d]_{5.10.5} & M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KB_\natural \ar[d]^{M\otimes_K\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}} \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE \ar[r]_{5.10.8} & M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KA_\natural. } \]

(The supplementary hypotheses on \(A\) and \(B\) (satisfied for example if \(A\) and \(B\) are \(K\)-algebras of finite groups) are doubtless unnecessary, and one should have a commutative square analogous to 5.10.10 with \(A_\natural\) (resp. \(B_\natural\)) replaced by \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\) (resp. \(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B\)).
