The first assertion of 5.10.9 is trivial. Let us prove the second. We may assume \(M\) with injective components and \(E\) with components of the form \(B\otimes_KL\), with \(L\) flat. We then have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_BE= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,B\otimes_KM)\otimes_BE\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KM) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,A\otimes_KM)\otimes_AE\), and the arrows of 5.10.10 are defined at the level of complexes. We are thus reduced to checking that, if \(L,M\) are \(K\)-modules, \(E\) a left \(B\)-module, we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{*} \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\otimes_KL,B\otimes_KM) \otimes_B(B\otimes_KL) \ar[r]^{5.3.3} \ar[d]_{(1)} & M\otimes_KB_\natural \ar[d]^{M\otimes_K\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}} \\ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B\otimes_KL,A\otimes_KM) \otimes_A(B\otimes_KL) \ar[r]_{5.3.3} & M\otimes_KA_\natural, } \]

where the arrow (1) is defined by sending \(B\otimes_KM\) into \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_AB\otimes_KM\) by 5.10.1 and composing with an isomorphism of type 5.10.3 and an adjunction isomorphism. Since \(A\) is flat and of finite presentation as a \(K\)-module, the "tensor product" arrows

\[ \tag{2}B\otimes_K\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(L,M) =\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B,B)\otimes_K \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(L,M) \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B\otimes_KL,B\otimes_KM), \] \[ \tag{3}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_K \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(L,M) \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B\otimes_KL,A\otimes_KM) \]

are isomorphisms, by which (1) identifies with the tensor product (over \(K\)) of 5.10.1 by \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(L,M)\otimes_KL\). On the other hand, the upper horizontal arrow (resp. lower of (*)) identifies by (2) (resp. (3)) with the tensor product (over \(K\))

of \(\operatorname{Tr}_B:B\longrightarrow B_\natural\) (resp. \(\operatorname{ev}:\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,A)\otimes_A B \longrightarrow A_\natural\)) by the evaluation arrow \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_K(L,M)\otimes_KL\longrightarrow M\). The commutativity of (*) thus follows from that of 5.10.2, which completes the proof of 5.10.9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10.11 — Under the hypotheses of 5.10.9, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(B)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\), we have a commutative square of \(D(K)\)
\[ \tag{5.10.12} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B}\ar[d] & M\otimes_KB_\natural\ar[d]\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_A} & M\otimes_KA_\natural, } \]

where the left vertical arrow is the restriction of scalars.
It suffices indeed to combine 5.10.6 and 5.10.9.
5.11. Application to finite groups: divisibility properties.

Let \(G\) be a finite group, with identity element denoted \(e\), and set \(K[G]=B\). The \(K\)-module \(B_ atural\) is free with basis the set \(G_ atural\) of conjugacy classes of \(G\) ; every section \(s\) of \(B_ atural\) can be written in a unique way \(s=\sum_{x\in G_ atural}s(x)x\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.11.1 — (cf. SGA 41/2 Rapport 4.5). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(B)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\). For \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE)\), we have, in \(H^0(T,M)\),
\[\tag{*}\operatorname{Tr}_K(u)=|G|\,\operatorname{Tr}_B(u)(e).\]

(*)

It suffices indeed to apply 5.10.11 with \(A=K\), noting that \(\operatorname{Tr}_{B/K}:B_\natural\longrightarrow K\) is given by

\[ \tag{5.11.2} \operatorname{Tr}_{B/K}(g)= \begin{cases}|G|&\text{if }g=e,\\0&\text{if }g\ne e. \end{cases} \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.11.3 — Under the hypotheses of 5.11.1, let \(g\in G\), denote by \(Z_g\) the centralizer of \(g\) in \(G\), and set \(A=K[Z_g]\). Let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE)\), and denote by \(gu\in \operatorname{Hom}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE)\) the composite of the image of \(u\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE)\) with left multiplication by \(g\). Then we have, in \(H^0(T,M)\),
\[\operatorname{Tr}_A(gu)(e)=\operatorname{Tr}_B(u)(g^{-1}).\]

We shall show more generally that we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.11.4} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B}\ar[d] & M\otimes_KB_\natural \ar[r]^-{x\mapsto x(g^{-1})} & M\ar@{=}[d]\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_A} & M\otimes_KA_\natural \ar[r]_-{x\mapsto x(e)} & M, } \]

where the left vertical arrow is the composite of the restriction of scalars and of left multiplication by \(g\). This square is the boundary of the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{rest}} & M\otimes_KB_\natural \ar[r]^-{x\mapsto x(g^{-1})}\ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}} & M\ar@{=}[dd]\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A}\ar[d]_{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,s_g)} & M\otimes_KA_\natural\ar[d]^{s_g}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Tr}_A} & M\otimes_KA_\natural \ar[r]_-{x\mapsto x(e)} & M, } \]

where rest denotes the restriction of scalars and \(s_g\) left multiplication by \(g\). The square (1) commutes by virtue of 5.10.11, and (2) by definition of \(\operatorname{Tr}_A\). It thus suffices to prove the commutativity of (3), and for that it suffices to show that, for \(h\in G\), we have

\[ \operatorname{Tr}_A(x\longmapsto gxh)(e)=\text{value at }g^{-1} \text{ of the image of }h\text{ in }B_\natural, \]

i.e.

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Tr}_A(x\longmapsto gxh)(e)= \begin{cases}1&\text{if }g^{-1}\text{ is conjugate to }h,\\0&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases} \]

For this we may replace \(T\) by the punctual topos and \(K\) by \(\mathbb Z\), and it then suffices to prove (*) after multiplication by \(|Z_g|\). Taking account of 5.11.1, we are thus reduced to showing that

\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z}(x\longmapsto gxh)= \begin{cases}|Z_g|&\text{if }g^{-1}\text{ is conjugate to }h,\\0&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases} \]

But this follows from the fact that the first member is the number of fixed points of the map \(x\longmapsto gxh\) from \(G\) into \(G\), whence the commutativity of 5.11.4 and the conclusion of 5.11.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.11.5 — Under the hypotheses of 5.11.3, we have
\[\operatorname{Tr}_K(gu)=|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_B(u)(g^{-1}) =|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_A(gu)(e).\]
5.12. Traces and external tensor products.

Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(A_i\) be a flat \(K\)-algebra, \(A=A_1\otimes_KA_2\). Let \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A_i)\), \(F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A_i^e)\) be such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_i}(E_i,F_i)\in \operatorname{ob}D^b(A_i^o)\), \(F=F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF_2 \in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^o)\), where \(E=E_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE_2\). We then have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.12.1} \xymatrix{ (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1}E_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_2}(E_2,F_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}E_2) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)} & (F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1^e}A_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K(F_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2^e}A_2) \ar[d]^{(3)}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE \ar[r] & F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A, } \]
where (1) is the tensor product of the arrows 5.3.5, (2) is defined by the tensor product arrow
\[ \tag{5.12.2} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_2}(E_2,F_2) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F), \]

and (3) is the obvious canonical isomorphism. The verification is essentially trivial. One checks likewise that, for \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A_i)\), \(F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_i^e)\), \(G_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A_i)\), with \(F_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_i}G_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A_i)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_i}(E_i,F_i)\in \operatorname{ob}D^b(A_i^o)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_i}(E_i,F_i)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_i}G_i \in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\), \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG\in \operatorname{ob}D^b(A)\) (where \(G=G_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KG_2\)), we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.12.3} \xymatrix{ (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1}G_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_2}(E_2,F_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}G_2) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1}G_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_2}(E_2,F_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}G_2) \ar[d]^{(3)}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG \ar[r]_{5.4.2} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG), } \]

where (2) and (3) are tensor product arrows of type 5.12.2, and (1) is the tensor product of the arrows 5.4.2. From the commutativity of the squares 5.12.1 and 5.12.3 it follows that, for \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(A_i)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A_i^e)\) such that \(F=F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KF_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A^e)\), we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{5.12.4} \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1}E_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_2}(E_2,F_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}E_2) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)} & (F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1^e}A_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_K(F_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2^e}A_2) \ar[d]^{(3)}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE) \ar[r]_{5.6.1} & F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A, } \]

where (1) is the tensor product of the arrows \(\operatorname{Tr}_{A_i}\) (5.6.1), (2) is defined by 5.12.2, and (3) is the canonical isomorphism appearing in 5.12.1. Consequently, for \(u_i\in\operatorname{Hom}_{A_i}(E_i,F_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_i}E_i)\), we have
\[ \tag{5.12.5} \operatorname{Tr}_A(u_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Ku_2) =\operatorname{Tr}_{A_1}(u_1)\operatorname{Tr}_{A_2}(u_2), \]
the product in the second member being defined by the canonical arrow
\[ H^0(T,F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1^e}A_1)\otimes H^0(T,F_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2^e}A_2) \longrightarrow H^0(T,F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A). \]
5.13. Traces of semi-linear endomorphisms.

Let \(A\) be a \(K\)-algebra, \(\varphi\) an endomorphism of \(A\). If \(E,F\) are left \(A\)-modules, recall that an additive homomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) is said to be \(\varphi\)-\(A\)-linear if it satisfies \(u(ax)=\varphi(a)u(x)\) for all \(a\in A\), \(x\in E\), i.e. if \(u\) is an \(A\)-linear homomorphism of \(E\) into the \(A\)-module \({}_{(\varphi)}F\) deduced from \(F\) by restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). If \(F\) is an \(A\)-bimodule,

\(G\) a left \(A\)-module, we have an obvious isomorphism \({}_{(\varphi)}F\otimes_AG\xrightarrow{\sim}{}_{(\varphi)}(F\otimes_AG)\), which derives (trivially) to \({}_{(\varphi)}F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG\xrightarrow{\sim} {}_{(\varphi)}(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG)\).
Suppose \(A\) flat over \(K\). From 5.4.2 we then deduce an arrow

\[ \tag{5.13.1} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,{}_{(\varphi)}F) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,{}_{(\varphi)}(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG)) \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A^e)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\), with \(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AG\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A)\). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), 5.13.1 is an isomorphism, which allows us to define, by 5.3.5, a trace arrow

\[ \tag{5.13.2} \operatorname{Tr}_{A,\varphi}:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A (E,{}_{(\varphi)}(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_AE)) \longrightarrow{}_{(\varphi)}F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A. \]
From this we deduce in particular, for \(F=A\), an arrow
\[ \tag{5.13.3} \operatorname{Tr}_{A,\varphi}:\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,{}_{(\varphi)}E) \longrightarrow H^0(T,A_{\natural,\varphi}) \]

where \(A_{\natural,\varphi}={}_{(\varphi)}A\otimes_{A^e}A\) is the quotient \(K\)-module of \(A\) by the submodule generated locally by the local sections of the form \(ab-\varphi(b)a\). In the case where \(A=K[G]\), \(G\) a discrete group, with \(\varphi\) defined by an endomorphism \(\varphi\) of \(G\), \(A_{\natural,\varphi}\) is the free \(K\)-module with basis \(G_{\natural,\varphi}\), where \(G_{\natural,\varphi}\) is the quotient of \(G\) by the action \((g,x)\longmapsto\varphi(g)xg^{-1}\) (the orbits are sometimes called "\(\varphi\)-conjugacy classes" of \(G\)). The traces 5.14.2 coincide, for \(T\) punctual, with those considered by Grothendieck, and which are discussed in Exposé XII.

In the situation of 5.10, if \(\varphi\), \(\psi\) are respective endomorphisms of \(A\), \(B\), compatible with \(A\longrightarrow B\), there exists a unique arrow \(\operatorname{Tr}_A:B_{\natural,\psi}\longrightarrow A_{\natural,\varphi}\) making commutative the square (analogous to 5.10.2)

\[ \tag{5.13.4} \xymatrix{ {{}_{(\psi)}B=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(B,{}_{(\psi)}B)} \ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{B,\psi}}\ar[d] & B_{\natural,\psi} \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A}\\ {\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(B,{}_{(\varphi)}A)\otimes_AB} \ar[r]_{\operatorname{ev}} & A_{\natural,\varphi} }, \]
where \(\operatorname{év}\) is the evaluation arrow, and the left vertical arrow is the restriction of scalars. One defines an arrow analogous to 5.10.5, with \(A\otimes_KM\) (resp.
\(B\otimes_KM\)) replaced by \({}_{(\varphi)}A\otimes_KM\) (resp. \({}_{(\psi)}B\otimes_KM\)), and we have compatibilities analogous to 5.10.6, 5.10.9, 5.10.11, which we leave to the reader to state.

Let \(G\) be a finite group, \(\varphi\) an endomorphism of \(G\). For \(g\in G\), denote by \(Z_{g,\varphi}\) the \(\varphi\)-centralizer of \(g\) in \(G\), i.e. the set of \(x\in G\) such that \(\varphi(x)gx^{-1}=g\). Set \(A=K[G]\), and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(N\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(K)\). One easily deduces from the preceding compatibilities that we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,{}_{(\varphi)}(M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_KE))\), \(g\in G\),
\[ \tag{5.13.5} \operatorname{Tr}_K(gu)=|Z_{g^{-1},\varphi}|\operatorname{Tr}_{A,\varphi}(u)(g^{-1}), \]
(the details of the verification are left to the reader).

footnotes.html:
If \(M\) is a sheaf, the notation
\(x\in M\) means that \(x\) is a local section of \(M\).

In no. 6, we shall apply the
constructions that follow to the case where \(M\) is a dualizing complex (6.7).

If \(X\) is a set, we denote by
\(|X|\) the cardinal of \(X\).

pages.title: 6.- Equivariant correspondences and divisibility of local terms.

blocks.html: 6.- Equivariant correspondences and divisibility of local terms.
6.1. Notations.

In this section, \(S\) denotes the spectrum of a field of char. \(p\), and \(\Lambda\) a commutative noetherian ring killed by an integer not divisible by \(p\). The \(S\)-schemes considered will be assumed separated and of finite type. The \(\Lambda\)-algebras considered will be associative, unital, and of finite type, but not necessarily commutative. Most often, and in particular for everything concerning the "noncommutative Lefschetz-Verdier local terms", they will be assumed flat (in the applications, they will be algebras of finite groups). If \(X\) is an \(S\)-scheme, and \(A\) a \(\Lambda\)-algebra, we shall denote by \(D({}_A X)\) (resp. \(D(X_A)\)) the derived category of the category of sheaves of left (resp. right) \(A\)-modules on \(X\) ; for \(A\) commutative, we shall also write \(D(X,A)\) for \(D({}_A X)\), and \(D(X)=D(X,\Lambda)\). We shall denote by \(D_c(-)\) (resp. \(D_{ctf}(-)\)) the full subcategory formed of the complexes with constructible cohomology (resp. of finite tor-dimension and with constructible cohomology). If \(A\), \(B\) are \(\Lambda\)-algebras, we shall denote by \(D({}_A X_B)\) the derived category of that of sheaves of \((A,B)\)-bimodules (left over \(A\), right over \(B\)). Finally, we shall use the notations \(A^o\), \(A^e\), \(A_\natural\) of 5.1.
The formalism of (III 1, 2, 3) extends without difficulty to the "noncommutative" case. Let us indicate rapidly the principal points of this generalization.

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(A\), \(B\) \(\Lambda\)-algebras. If \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_A X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D({}_A X_B)\), \(M\) being of finite tor-dimension and with constructible cohomology as an object of \(D(X_B)\), then \(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_B)\) (the verification is immediate). On the other hand, it follows from (SGA 41/2 Finitude 1.6) that, for \(M\) as above, and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_A X)\), we have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(L,M)\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_B)\). It also follows from (loc. cit.) that, if \(f\) is an \(S\)-morphism, \(D_{ctf}({}_A-)\) is stable under the operations \((f^*,f_*,f_!,f^!)\) (the case of \(f^*\) being trivial).
Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism,

\(f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X=X_1\times_SX_2\longrightarrow Y=Y_1\times_SY_2\), \(A\) a \(\Lambda\)-algebra. For \(L_1\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_{1A})\), \(L_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_A X_2)\), we have a Künneth arrow (in \(D^-(Y)\))
\[ \tag{6.2.1} f_{1*}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}f_{2*}L_2 \longrightarrow f_*(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2) \]

(where \(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}= p_1^*L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Ap_2^*L_2\)), of which one deduces from (loc. cit.), as in (III 1.6), that it is an isomorphism. If \(B\), \(C\) are \(\Lambda\)-algebras, with \(B\) or \(C\) assumed flat over \(\Lambda\) (in order to be able to define \(\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A:D^-({}_B-A)\times D^-({}_A-C) \longrightarrow D^-({}_B-C)\)), we have an isomorphism analogous to 6.2.1, in \(D^-({}_B Y_C)\), for \(L_1\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_B X_{1A})\), \(L_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_A X_{2C})\). One checks likewise that, for \(M_1\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y_{1A})\), \(M_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_A Y_2)\), we have a Künneth isomorphism (in \(D^-(X)\))
\[ \tag{6.2.2} f_1^!M_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}f_2^!M_2 \longrightarrow f^!(M_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}M_2) \]

(and an analogous isomorphism in \(D^-({}_B X_C)\) for \(M_1\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_B Y_{1A})\), \(M_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^-({}_A Y_{2C})\)). Finally, let, for \(i=1,2\), \(A_i\) be a \(\Lambda\)-algebra, \(A_2\) being flat, \(E_1\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{A_1}X_1)\), \(F_2\in\operatorname{ob}D^+({}_{A_2}X_2)\), \(F_1\in\operatorname{ob}D({}_{A_1}X_{1A_2})\), with \(F_1\) of finite tor-dimension and with constructible cohomology as an object of \(D(X_{1A_2})\). Then we have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1)\in \operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}^-(X_{1A_2})\), as we saw above, and we have a canonical arrow of \(D^+(X)\) (\(D^+(X,A_2)\) when \(A_2\) is commutative)

\[ \tag{6.2.3} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A_2}F_2\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(p_1^*E_1, p_1^*F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A_2}F_2), \]
defined by composition of the "inverse image" arrow (III 2.1.1)

\[ p_1^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(p_1^*E_1,p_1^*F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2 \]
with the arrow analogous to 5.4.2

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(p_1^*E_1,p_1^*F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(p_1^*E_1, p_1^*F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2) \]
(adjoint according to 5.2.3 of the arrow

\[ p_1^*E_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_1} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(p_1^*E_1,p_1^*F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2\longrightarrow p_1^*F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A_2}p_2^*F_2 \]
defined by the evaluation arrow 5.3.6). One deduces from 6.2.1, as in (III 2.3),
that 6.2.3 is an isomorphism. In particular, for \(A_2=\Lambda\), \(F_2=\Lambda_{X_2}\), 6.2.3 gives an isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.2.4} p_1^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1}(E_1,F_1) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A_1} (p_1^*E_1,p_1^*F_1). \]
If \(t:T\longrightarrow S\) is an \(S\)-scheme, and \(A\) a \(\Lambda\)-algebra, we shall set
\[ \tag{6.3.1} t^!A=KA_T\qquad(\in\operatorname{ob}D^+({}_A T_A)). \]
For \(A=\Lambda\), we shall write \(K_T\) instead of \(K\Lambda_T\). It follows from 6.2.2 that we have a canonical isomorphism (of \(D^+({}_A T_A)\))
\[ \tag{6.3.2} K_T\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}A\xrightarrow{\sim}KA_T. \]

We shall denote by \(D_A\) the functor \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(-,KA_T)\) : it sends \(D_{ctf}({}_A T)\) into \(D_{ctf}(T_A)\) (and vice-versa), and the proof of (SGA 41/2 Th. Finitude 4) shows that, for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_A T)\), the canonical arrow \(L\longrightarrow D_A D_A L\) is an isomorphism. For \(A=\Lambda\), we shall write \(D\) instead of \(D_A\).

Let \(A\) be a flat \(\Lambda\)-algebra, and let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) the projections, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_A X_i)\). By 6.2.3 applied to \(A_1=A_2=A\), \(E_1=L_1\), \(F_2=L_2\), \(F_1=KA_{X_1}\), we have an isomorphism
\[ D_A L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L_1, KA_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2), \]
whence, composing with the Künneth isomorphism 6.2.2 \(KA_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2\xrightarrow{\sim}p_2^!L_2\), an isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.4.1} D_A L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2), \]

which generalizes (III 3.1.1). Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) be a \(S\)-morphism, and set \(c_i=p_ic\). By the induction formula (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.2), we have a canonical isomorphism \(analogue à (III 3.2.1)\)
\[ \tag{6.4.2} c^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2), \]
whence, composing with 6.4.1, an isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.4.3} H^0(X,c_*c^!(D_A L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L_2)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2). \]

The elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\) will be called cohomological correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in \(C\) (the terminology differs slightly from that of (loc. cit.); the two terminologies coincide for \(c\) proper, which is the case in practice).

Given \(S\)-morphisms \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) and a commutative square (III 3.7.1) with \(c\) proper, one defines as in (loc. cit.) "direct image" arrows analogous to (III 3.7.5), (III 3.7.6) (for \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(A_{X_i})\)).

Let \(A\) be a flat \(\Lambda\)-algebra, \(X\) an \(S\)-scheme, \(X^2=X\times_S X\), \(p_1,p_2:X^2\rightrightarrows X\) the canonical projections, \(\delta:X\longrightarrow X^2\) the diagonal, \(c:C\longrightarrow X^2\) an \(S\)-morphism, \(c_i=p_ic\), \(X^c=C\times_{X^2}X\) the scheme of fixed points of \(c\), whence a cartesian square
\[ \tag{6.5.1} \xymatrix{X^c\ar[r]^{i^c}\ar[d]_{\delta^c}&X\ar[d]^{\delta}\\ C\ar[r]_c&X^2.} \]
Set
\[ \tag{6.5.2} KA_{X,L\natural}=K_X\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A), \qquad KA_{X,\natural}=K_X\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}A_{\natural}. \]
Let \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(A_X)\). We define a "local trace" homomorphism
\[ \tag{6.5.3} \operatorname{Tr}_A:\delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L,p_2^!L) \longrightarrow KA_{X,L\natural} \]
by composing the isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.5.4} \delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L,p_2^!L) \xrightarrow{\sim}\delta^*(D_A L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L) \]
deduced from the inverse of 6.4.1 (for \(X_1=X_2\), \(L_1=L_2\)) by application of \(\delta^*\), the trivial isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.5.5} \delta^*(D_A L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A}L)=D_A L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A L, \]
and the evaluation arrow 5.3.5
\[ \tag{6.5.6} D_A L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A L\longrightarrow KA_{X,L\natural} \]

(where we have identified \(KA_X\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\) with \(KA_{X,L\natural}\) thanks to 6.3.2 \(K_X\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}A=KA_X\)). It follows at once from the definition that, for \(X=S\), 6.5.3 coincides with the arrow \(\operatorname{Tr}_A:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(L,L)\longrightarrow A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\) of 5.6.1. Composing the arrow deduced from 6.5.3 by application of \(i_*^ci^{c!}\) with the arrow
\[ \tag{6.5.7} \delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L) \longrightarrow i_*^ci^{c!}\delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L,p_2^!L) \]
deduced from 6.4.2 and from the base change arrow \(\delta^*c_*c^!\longrightarrow i_*^ci^{c!}\delta^*\), we obtain an arrow
\[ \tag{6.5.8} \operatorname{Tr}_A:\delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L) \longrightarrow i_*^ci^{c!}KA_{X,L\natural}=i_*^cKA_{X^c,L\natural}, \]
whence the arrow
\[ \tag{6.5.9} \operatorname{Tr}_A:\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L) \longrightarrow H^0(X^c,KA_{X^c,L\natural}). \]

We shall again denote by \(\operatorname{Tr}_A\) the arrows composed of 6.5.3, 6.5.8, 6.5.9 with the arrows deduced from the canonical arrow \(KA_{X,L\natural}\longrightarrow KA_{X,\natural}\). One easily checks that, for \(A=\Lambda\), we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\),
\[ \tag{6.5.10}\operatorname{Tr}(u)=\langle u,1\rangle, \]

with the notation of (III 4.2.5), \(1\) denoting the diagonal correspondence (more precisely, the arrow \(c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L) \longrightarrow\delta_*i_*^cK_{X^c}\) adjoint to 6.5.8 is the one defined by (III 4.2.4') (with \(X_1=X_2\), \(L_1=L_2\), \(d=\delta\)) and the diagonal correspondence \(1\in\operatorname{Hom}(L,L)\)).
Let \(A\) be a flat \(\Lambda\)-algebra, and, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) a \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(A_{X_i})\). We define a pairing
\[ \tag{6.6.1} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_2^*L_2,p_1^!L_1) \longrightarrow KA_{X,L\natural} \]
in the following way. We identify the first member with

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L_1,p_1^*KA_{X_1}) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A p_2^*L_2 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_2^*L_2,p_2^*KA_{X_2}) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A p_1^*L_1 \]
by the isomorphisms 6.4.1, then with

\[ A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A( , ) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}p_2^*L_2 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A( , ) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}p_1^*L_1 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A \]

by 5.3.1; we send this object into \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}(KA_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda} KA_{X_2})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A=R\) by the tensor product of evaluation arrows 5.3.6; we finally identify \(R\) with \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}(KA_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda} KA_{X_2})\) by 5.8.2, then with \(KA_{X,L\natural}\) by 6.5.2 and (III 1.7.6). If \(c:C\longrightarrow X\), \(d:D\longrightarrow X\) are correspondences, and \(e=c\times_Xd:E\longrightarrow X\), we deduce from 6.6.1, as in (III 4.2), a pairing

\[ \tag{6.6.2} c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda} d_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1) \longrightarrow e_*KA_{E,L\natural}, \]
whence a "cup-product"
\[ \tag{6.6.3} \langle\ ,\ \rangle_A:\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}_A(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\longrightarrow H^0(E,KA_{E,L\natural}). \]

It is not difficult to extend to the noncommutative case the notion of composition of correspondences studied in (III 5.2), and one checks that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\), we have the formula analogous to (III 5.2.9)
\[ \tag{6.6.4}\langle u,v\rangle_A=\operatorname{Tr}_A(vu)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(uv). \]

The Lefschetz formula (III 4.4) generalizes without difficulty in this context: given proper \(S\)-morphisms \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) (\(i=1,2\)), and a commutative cube (III 4.4.0), we have, for \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(A_{X_i})\), a commutative square analogous to (III 4.4.1), with \(K_C\) (resp. \(K_D\)) replaced by \(KA_{C,L\natural}\) (resp. \(KA_{D,L\natural}\)), and a formula analogous to (III 4.5.1), in \(H^0(D,KA_{D,L\natural})\) :
\[ \tag{6.6.5}\langle f_*u,f_*v\rangle_A=g_*\langle u,v\rangle_A \]
(the arrow \(f_*c_*KA_{C,L\natural}\longrightarrow d_*KA_{D,L\natural}\) is deduced from the arrow (4) of (III 4.4.1) by tensorization with \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\)).

Let \(X\) and \(c\) be as in 6.5, and \(A\longrightarrow B\) a morphism of flat \(\Lambda\)-algebras. With respect to extension and restriction of scalars, the arrows \(\operatorname{Tr}_A\) (6.5.3, 6.5.8, 6.5.9) behave like the arrows \(\operatorname{Tr}_A\) of 5.6.1, 5.6.2. The point is that, if \(f\) is an \(S\)-morphism, the functors \((f^*,f_*,f_!,f^!)\) commute with extension and restriction of scalars, and that consequently the study of the behavior of \(\operatorname{Tr}_A\) (6.5.3) with respect to extension and restriction of scalars reduces to that of the behavior of the "tensor product" arrow
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L,p_1^*KA_X) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A p_2^*L\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(p_1^*L,p_1^*KA_X \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A p_2^*L) \]
and of the "evaluation" arrow
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(L,KA_X)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A L \longrightarrow KA_{X,L\natural}. \]
Thus, the commutativity of the squares 5.9.1, 5.9.2 implies that of the square

\[ \tag{6.7.1} \xymatrix{ \delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A}\ar[d]& i_*^cKA_{X^c,L\natural}\ar[d]\\ \delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(c_1^*(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A L),c_2^!(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A L))\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B}& i_*^cKB_{X^c,L\natural},} \]

where the left vertical arrow is the extension of scalars and the right one is defined by the canonical arrow \(A\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{A^e}A\longrightarrow B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{B^e}B\) ; it follows that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\), we have
\[ \tag{6.7.2} \operatorname{Tr}_B(B\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A u)=\varphi\operatorname{Tr}_A(u), \]
where \(\varphi:H^0(X^c,KA_{X^c,L\natural})\longrightarrow H^0(X^c,KB_{X^c,L\natural})\) is the canonical map.

Suppose \(A\) of finite type and flat as a \(\Lambda\)-module, \(B\) of finite type and flat as a left \(A\)-module, and \(A_{ atural}\), \(B_{ atural}\) flat over \(\Lambda\). We have at our disposal \(\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}:B_{ atural}\longrightarrow A_{ atural}\), and we shall again denote by
\[ \tag{6.7.3} \operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}:KB_{X,\natural}\longrightarrow KA_{X,\natural}, \]

the arrow deduced from it by application of \(K_X\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda}-\) (6.5.2). It follows from 5.10.6 and 5.10.9 that, for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(B_X)\), we have a commutative square

\[ \tag{6.7.4} \xymatrix{ \delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_B}\ar[d]& i_*^cKB_{X^c,\natural}\ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}}\\ \delta^*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_A}& i_*^cKA_{X^c,\natural},} \]
where the left vertical arrow is the restriction of scalars. Consequently, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_B(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\), we have, in \(H^0(X^c,KA_{X^c,\natural})\),
\[ \tag{6.7.5} \operatorname{Tr}_A(u)=\operatorname{Tr}_{B/A}(\operatorname{Tr}_B(u)). \]

Let \(A,B\) be flat \(\Lambda\)-algebras, \(X,Y\) \(S\)-schemes, \(Z=X\times_S Y\), \(c:C\longrightarrow X^2\), \(d:D\longrightarrow Y^2\) correspondences, \(e=c\times_Sd:E=C\times_SD\longrightarrow Z^2\) the product. We thus have \(Z^e=X^c\times_SY^d\). Set \(R=A\otimes_\Lambda B\). We define as in (III 5.3) the external tensor product

\[ \tag{6.8.1} \operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\otimes\operatorname{Hom}_B(d_1^*M,d_2^!M) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_R(e_1^*(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM), e_2^!(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SM)), \]

and one easily deduces from the commutativity of the squares 5.12.1, 5.12.3, that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_B(d_1^*M,d_2^!M)\), we have, in \(H^0(Z^e,KR_{Z^e,L\natural})\),
\[ \tag{6.8.2} \operatorname{Tr}_R(u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Sv)= \operatorname{Tr}_A(u)\operatorname{Tr}_B(v), \]
the product in the second member being defined by the canonical arrow
\[ \tag{6.8.3} H^0(X^c,KA_{X^c,L\natural})\otimes H^0(Y^d,KB_{Y^d,L\natural}) \longrightarrow H^0(Z^e,KR_{Z^e,L\natural}), \]
coming from the isomorphism \(KA_{X^c,L\natural}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S KB_{Y^d,L\natural} \longrightarrow K(A\otimes B)_{Z^d,L\natural}\) given by (III 1.7.6) and the arrow (4) of 5.9.1).

Let \(G\) be a finite group. If \(X\) is a \(G\)-\(S\)-scheme (i.e. a \(S\)-scheme equipped with an action of \(G\) by \(S\)-automorphisms(*)), and \(A\) a \(\Lambda\)-algebra(**), we shall denote by \(D(G,{}_AX)\) the derived category of that of sheaves of left \(G\)-\(A\)-modules on \(X\) (for the notions of \(G\)-sheaf of sets, of rings, of modules, etc.) on \(X\) (for the étale topology), we refer the reader to (X 1)), and we

shall designate by the index \(c\) (resp. \(ctf\)) the full subcategory formed of the complexes of \(G\)-\(A\)-modules which, as complexes of \(A\)-modules, have constructible cohomology (resp. are of finite tor-dimension and with constructible cohomology). Likewise, if \(B\) is a \(\Lambda\)-algebra, we shall denote by \(D(G,{}_AX_B)\) the derived category of that of \(G\)-(\(A,B\))-bimodules on \(X\). If \(G\) acts trivially on \(X\), a left \(G\)-\(A\)-module on \(X\) is none other than a left \(A[G]\)-module on \(X\) (without group of operators), hence \(D(G,{}_AX)=D({}_{A[G]}X)\) ; since \(G\) is finite, we have moreover \(D_c({}_AX)=D_c({}_{A[G]}X)\), but we have only an inclusion \(D_{ctf}({}_{A[G]}X)\subset D_{ctf}(G,{}_AX)\).

The duality formalism of (SGA 4 XVII, XVIII) extends without difficulty to the case of \(G\)-\(S\)-schemes. Since the functor forgetting the action of \(G\) is faithful, it suffices to extend in a natural way the definition of the "six operations" (\(\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\), \(f^*\), \(f_*\), \(f_!\), \(f^!\)) and of the canonical arrows envisaged in (loc. cit.) (base change, Künneth, duality, etc.). This extension offers no difficulty (for the definition of the functors \(f_!\), \(f^!\), it suffices to use equivariant compactifications). We leave to the reader the task of paraphrasing the definitions and results of 6.2 to 6.8 in the setting of \(G\)-\(S\)-schemes. If, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) is a \(G\)-\(S\)-scheme, \(c:C\longrightarrow X=X_1\times_SX_2\) a \(G\)-\(S\)-morphism, and \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(G,{}_AX_i)\), the elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\) (\(\operatorname{Hom}_A=\operatorname{Hom}\) in the category \(D(G,{}_A-)\)) will be called equivariant (cohomological) correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in \(c\). Note that, when \(G\) acts trivially on \(X_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) and \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{A[G]}X_i)\), the isomorphism 6.4.1 (in \(D(G,X)\)) "refines" to an isomorphism
\[ D_{A[G]}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{S,A[G]}L_2 \mathbin{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{A[G]}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2). \]

It follows that, in a \(G\)-equivariant situation of type 6.5, with trivial action of \(G\) on the spaces, and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{A[G]}X)\subset D_{ctf}(G,{}_AX)\), one can, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{A[G]}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\), define a local term \(\operatorname{Tr}_{A[G]}(u)\in H^0(X^c,KA[G]_{X^c,L\natural})\), finer (in an obvious sense) than the local term \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(u)\in H^0(G,X^c,KA_{X^c,L\natural})\) obtained by using only the finite tor-dimension of \(L\) with respect to \(A\).
Let \(X,c\) be as in 6.5, and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}X)\). For \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\), we have, by 6.5.9, a local trace
\[ \tag{6.10.1} \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)\in H^0(X^c,K\Lambda[G]_{X^c,\natural}) =H^0(X^c;K_{X^c}\otimes\Lambda[G_\natural]), \]

where \(G_\natural\) is the set of conjugacy classes of \(G\), and for every \(x\in G_\natural\), one can consider the "value of \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)\) at \(x\)", \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)(x)\in H^0(X^c,K_{X^c})\), image of \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)\) under the projection \(H^0(X^c,K_{X^c}\otimes\Lambda[G_\natural])\longrightarrow H^0(X^c,K_{X^c})\) defined by \(x\). It follows from 6.7.5 and 5.11.2 that we have
\[ \tag{6.10.2} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(u)=|G|\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)(e), \]

where \(e\) denotes the identity element of \(G\). More generally, let \(g\in G\), denote by \(Z_g\) the centralizer of \(g\), and by \(gu\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\) the correspondence composed of \(u\) with left multiplication by \(g:c_1^*L\longrightarrow c_1^*L\). We have \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}X)\), and consequently 6.10.2 implies
\[ \tag{6.10.3} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gu)=|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gu)(e). \]
By the same reasoning as in 5.11.3, one deduces from 6.7.4 the formula
\[ \tag{6.10.4} \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gu)(e)= \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)(g^{-1}), \]
which allows one, if desired, to rewrite 6.10.3 in the form
\[ \tag{6.10.5} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gu)=|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(u)(g^{-1}). \]
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper \(G\)-\(S\)-morphism, and
\[ \tag{6.11.1} \xymatrix{X\times_SX& C\ar[l]_c\ar[d]\\ Y\times_SY\ar[u]^{f\times_Sf}&D\ar[l]^d} \]
a \(G\)-equivariant commutative square of \(G\)-\(S\)-schemes, with \(c\) and \(d\) proper. We assume that \(G\) acts trivially on \(Y\) and \(D\). We denote by
\[ \tag{6.11.2} f^{c/d}\ (\text{or }f^c):X^c\longrightarrow Y^d \]

the morphism induced by 6.11.1 on the schemes of fixed points of \(c\) and \(d\), with the notations of 6.5.1. Let \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(G,X)\), and \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\) a \(G\)-equivariant correspondence with support in \(c\), whence, by direct image, a \(G\)-equivariant correspondence \(f_*u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(d_1^*f_*L,d_2^!f_*L)\). For \(g\in G\), we shall denote by \(Z_g\) the centralizer of \(g\), and by
\[ \tag{6.11.3} gu\in\operatorname{Hom}((gc_1)^*L,c_2^!L) \]
the \(Z_g\)-equivariant correspondence with support in
\[ \tag{6.11.4} gc\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}(gc_1=c_1g,c_2):C\longrightarrow X\times_SX \]
defined as the composite of the arrows
\[ g^*(c_1^*L)\mathbin{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\alpha_g}}c_1^*L \mathbin{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{u}}c_2^!L, \]
where \(\alpha_g\) is the arrow giving the action of \(G\) on \(c_1^*L\). It is clear that we have

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.12. — Under the hypotheses of 6.11, we assume that we have \(f_*L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\). Then, for every \(g\in G\), we have
\[ \tag{6.12.1} f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gu)=|Z_g| \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*u)(g^{-1}), \]

where \(f_*^{gc}:H^0(X^{gc},K_{X^{gc}})\longrightarrow H^0(Y^d,K_{Y^d})\) is the "Gysin" morphism defined by the adjunction arrow \(f_*^{gc}K_{X^{gc}}=f_*^{gc}f^{gc!}K_{Y^d}\longrightarrow K_{Y^d}\).
By 6.10.5 applied to \(f_*u\), and 6.11.5, we have indeed
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(f_*(gu))=|Z_g| \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*u)(g^{-1}), \]
and consequently 6.12.1 follows from the Lefschetz formula (III 4.5.1).
Let \(\ell\) be a prime number \(\ne p\). For every integer \(n\geq1\), set \(\Lambda_n=\mathbb Z/\ell^n\). Under the hypotheses of 6.11, let \(L=(L_n)\) be a projective system

of objects of \(D_{ctf}(G,X,\Lambda_n)\) such that \(L_m\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \mathbin{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}L_n\) for \(m\geq n\), and let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L,c_2^!L)\) be a projective system of equivariant correspondences \(u_n\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_n,c_2^!L_n)\) such that, for \(m\geq n\), \(u_m\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n=u_n\) modulo the isomorphism \(L_m\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n=L_n\). By virtue of the compatibility of local traces with extension of scalars, the local terms (for \(g\in G\)) \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n}(gu_n)\in H^0(X^{gc},K\Lambda_{n,X^{gc}})\) define an element
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(gu)\in H^0(X^{gc},K_{\ell,X^{gc}}) \mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}\varprojlim_n H^0(X^{gc},K\Lambda_{n,X^{gc}}), \]
and the \(f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n}(gu_n)= \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n}(gf_*u_n)\) an element
\[ f_*\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(gu)\in H^0(Y^d,K_{\ell,Y^d}) \mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}\varprojlim_n H^0(Y^d,K\Lambda_{n,Y^d}). \]
Suppose moreover that \(f_*L_n\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda_n[G]}Y)\) for every \(n\). Then the local traces \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n[G]}(f_*u_n)\) likewise define an element
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]}(f_*u)\in H^0(Y^d,K\mathbb Z_\ell[G]_{Y^d,\natural}) \mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}\varprojlim_n H^0(Y^d,K\Lambda_n[G]_{Y^d,\natural}). \]
It follows from 6.12 that we have
\[ \tag{6.13.1} f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(gu)=|Z_g| \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]}(f_*u)(g^{-1}). \]

Let \(X\) be a \(G\)-\(S\)-scheme, \(j:U\longrightarrow X\) an equivariant open immersion, \(c:C\longrightarrow X\times_SX\) a morphism of \(G\)-\(S\)-schemes such that \(c_2(=p_2c):C\longrightarrow X\) is quasi-finite and of finite tor-dimension. We assume that \(c_1^{-1}(U)\subset c_2^{-1}(U)\) ; we thus have commutative squares

\[ \tag{6.14.1} \xymatrix{ U&c_1^{-1}(U)\ar[l]\ar[d]&c_2^{-1}(U)\ar[l]\ar[r]\ar[d]&U\ar[d]\\ X& C\ar[l]_{c_1}\ar[d]&C\ar[l]\ar[r]^{c_2}\ar[d]&X\ar[d]\\ X-U&c_1^{-1}(X-U)\ar[l]&c_2^{-1}(X-U)\ar[l]\ar[r]&X-U.} \]
The trace morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}:c_{2!}c_2^!\Lambda_C\longrightarrow\Lambda_X\) then defines a filtered \(G\)-equivariant correspondence
\[ \tag{6.14.2} \underline c\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*\Lambda_X,c_2^!\Lambda_X), \]

\(\Lambda_X\) being equipped with the filtration defined by the submodule \(i_!\Lambda_U\) (i.e. \(F^n\Lambda_X=\Lambda_X\) for \(n\leq0\), \(F^1\Lambda_X=i_!\Lambda_U\), \(F^n\Lambda_X=0\) for \(n\geq1\)). The associated graded object consists of the two \(G\)-equivariant correspondences

\[ \tag{6.14.3} \operatorname{gr}^0\underline c=\underline c_{X-U,X} \in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*(\Lambda_{X-U,X}),c_2^!(\Lambda_{X-U,X})), \] \[ \operatorname{gr}^1\underline c=\underline c_{U,X} \in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*(\Lambda_{U,X}),c_2^!(\Lambda_{X-U,X})), \]
where we have set \(\Lambda_{X-U,X}=j_*\Lambda_{X-U}\), \(\Lambda_{U,X}=i_!\Lambda_U\). We have
\[ \tag{6.14.4} \underline c_{X-U,U}=j_*\underline c_{X-U}, \]

where \(\underline c_{X-U}\in \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*\Lambda_{X-U},c_2^!\Lambda_{X-U})\) is the correspondence defined by \(\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2,X-U}\). We shall write \(\underline c_\Lambda\), \(\underline c_{U,X}\), etc., when we wish to specify the base ring.
Let us forget the action of \(G\). We have local terms
\[ \tag{6.14.5} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c),\quad \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{U,X}),\quad \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{X-U,X}) \in H^0(X^c,K_{X^c}), \]
which, by virtue of the additivity of traces (III 4.13.1), satisfy the relation
\[ \tag{6.14.6} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c)= \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{U,X})+ \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{X-U,X}). \]
Moreover, by 6.14.4, we have
\[ \tag{6.14.7} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{X-U,X})= j_*^c\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{X-U}), \]

where \(j_*^c:H^0((X-U)^c,K_{(X-U)^c})\longrightarrow H^0(X^c,K_{X^c})\) is the direct image morphism defined by \(j^c:(X-U)^c\longrightarrow X^c\). Recall (III 4.3) that if \(X\) is smooth, \(c\) a closed immersion and \(X^c\) finite, \(\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c)\) is the image in \(\Lambda\) of the intersection multiplicity \((C.X)\) of \(C\) with the diagonal; if moreover \(X-U\) is smooth, \(\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(\underline c_{X-U})\) is the image in \(\Lambda\) of \((c_2^{-1}(X-U).(X-U))\). Note also that, if \(X-U\) is proper over \(S\), and \(c|X-U\) is the diagonal, then the Lefschetz formula
(III 4.7) implies that \(\int_{X-U}\operatorname{Tr}(\underline c_{X-U})\in\Lambda\) is the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(X-U\) (with values in \(\Lambda\)).

Let there be given a \(G\)-equivariant diagram 6.11.1 (with \(f,c,d\) proper), and \(U\longrightarrow X\) a \(G\)-equivariant open immersion. We assume that \(c_2\) is quasi-finite and of finite tor-dimension, \(c_1^{-1}(U)\subset c_2^{-1}(U)\), that \(G\) acts trivially on \(C\) and \(D\), and that \(f|U:U\longrightarrow f(U)=V\) is a Galois étale covering with group \(G\). This last hypothesis implies that \(Rf_*(\Lambda_{U,X})\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) ; in fact \(Rf_*(\Lambda_{U,X})\) is concentrated in degree 0 and extended by zero from a sheaf on \(V\) locally isomorphic to \(\Lambda[G]\). We can therefore apply 6.12 to \(L=\Lambda_{U,X}\), \(u=\underline c_{U,X}\), and, taking account of 6.14.6, 6.14.7, we obtain the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.16. — Under the hypotheses of 6.15, we have, for every \(g\in G\),
\[ \tag{6.16.1} f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(g\underline c)- (fj)_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(g\underline c_{X-U}) =|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*\underline c_{U,X})(g^{-1}), \]

where \(j:X-U\longrightarrow X\) is the inclusion, and \(Z_g\) denotes the centralizer of \(g\).

Applying 6.16 to the projective system of correspondences \(\underline c_\ell=(\underline c^{\Lambda_n})\), where \(\Lambda_n=\mathbb Z/\ell^n\) (\(\ell\) prime \(\ne p\)), and taking account of the fact that 6.14.6, 6.14.7 give, by passage to the limit and application of \(f_*^{gc}\),

\[ f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g\underline c_\ell)= f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g\underline c_{\ell,U,X})+ (fj)_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g\underline c_{\ell,X-U}), \]
we deduce from it, by 6.13,

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.17. — Under the hypotheses of 6.15, we have, for every \(g\in G\),
\[ \tag{6.17.1} f_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g\underline c_\ell)- (fj)_*^{gc}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g\underline c_{\ell,X-U}) =|Z_g|\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]}(f_*\underline c_{\ell,U,X})(g^{-1}), \]

with the notations of 6.16.

Taking account of the compatibility of local traces with localization and of the remarks made in 6.14, we see that 6.17 resolves affirmatively, in a more general formulation, the divisibility conjecture of Grothendieck (XII 4.5), in the case where the endomorphism \(\varphi\) of \(G\) considered in (loc. cit.) is the identity.

In fact, it is not difficult, taking inspiration from 5.13, to give divisibility statements analogous to 6.12, 6.13, 6.16, 6.17, in the case where \(c\) is not \(G\)-equivariant, but such that \(c_2\) is and that \(c_1\) satisfies \(c_1(xg)=c_1(x)\varphi(g)\), for every point \(x\) of \(G\), \(\varphi\) being a given endomorphism of \(G\) : the divisibility formulas are written as above, with \(Z_g\) replaced by the \(\varphi\)-centralizer of \(g^{-1}\) (5.13.5).
Set
\[ \tag{6.18.1} N_G=\sum_{g\in G}g\in\Lambda[G]. \]
Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme. For \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D({}_{\Lambda[G]}X)\), left multiplication by \(N_G\) induces an arrow of \(D(X)\)
\[ \tag{6.18.2} N_G:\Lambda\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}L \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(\Lambda,L), \]
where \(\Lambda\) is considered as a \(\Lambda[G]\)-algebra via the natural augmentation (sending every \(g\in G\) to 1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html: Lemma 6.18.3. — If \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}X)\), the arrow 6.18.2 is an isomorphism.

By dévissage we reduce indeed to assuming \(L\) perfect, then \(L=\Lambda[G]\), and the assertion follows from the fact that \(N_G\) (6.18.1) is a basis of the module of invariants under \(G\) of the left \(\Lambda[G]\)-module \(\Lambda[G]\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.19. — In the situation of 6.11, let \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*M,d_2^!M)\), \(a:M\longrightarrow f_*L\) be an arrow of \(D({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) (\(M\) being regarded as an object of \(D({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) via the augmentation \(\Lambda[G]\longrightarrow \Lambda\)) such that the square
\[ \xymatrix{ d_1^*M\ar[r]^v\ar[d]_{d_1^*a}&d_2^!M\ar[d]^{d_2^!a}\\ d_1^*f_*L\ar[r]_{f_*u}&d_2^!f_*L} \]

is commutative. We assume that \(f_*M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) and that \(a\) induces an isomorphism \(M\xrightarrow{\sim}R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(\Lambda,f_*L)\). Then we have
\[ \tag{6.19.1} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(v)= \sum_{g\in G_\natural}\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*u)(g^{-1}). \]

By 6.18.3, \(a\), composed with \(N_G^{-1}\), gives an isomorphism \(M\xrightarrow{\sim}\Lambda\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}f_*L\), by which \(v\) identifies with \(\Lambda\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}f_*u\). By 6.7.2, \(\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(v)\) is therefore the image of \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*u)\) under the arrow induced by the augmentation \(\Lambda[G]\longrightarrow\Lambda\) ; now the latter, by definition, sends each conjugacy class to 1, whence the conclusion.

Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be a \(G\)-\(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) a \(G\)-\(S\)-morphism such that \(c_2\) is quasi-finite and of finite tor-dimension. Let \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(G,X_i)\). The trace morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}:c_{2!}c_2^*L_2\longrightarrow L_2\) defines by adjunction an arrow (of \(D(G,X)\)) again denoted
\[ \tag{6.20.1} \operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}:c_2^*L_2\longrightarrow c_2^!L_2, \]
which permits associating to every (\(G\)-equivariant) morphism \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^*L_2)\) a (\(G\)-equivariant) correspondence
\[ \tag{6.20.2} u'=\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}\circ u\in \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.21. — Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper \(G\)-\(S\)-morphism, and an equivariant commutative square 6.11.1, with trivial action of \(G\) on \(Y\) and \(D\). We assume \(c,d\) proper, \(c_2,d_2\) quasi-finite and of finite tor-dimension, and the square
\[ \xymatrix{C\ar[r]^{c_2}\ar[d]_h&X\ar[d]^f\\D\ar[r]_{d_2}&Y} \]

cartesian and tor-independent (SGA 6 III 1.5). Let \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*M,d_2^*M)\), whence a \(G\)-equivariant homomorphism \(h^*v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*f^*M,c_2^*f^*M)\), and \(G\)-equivariant correspondences \((h^*v)'\in \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*f^*M,c_2^!f^*M)\), \(f_*((h^*v)')\in\operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*(f_*f^*M),d_2^!(f_*f^*M))\). We then have a commutative square of arrows of \(D({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) (\(M\) being considered as an object of \(D({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\) via the trivial action)
where the vertical arrows are given by the adjunction arrow \(M\longrightarrow f_*f^*M\).
Set \(h^*v=u\), \(f^*M=L\), and consider the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ d_1^*M\ar[r]^v\ar[d]_{(1)}&d_2^*M\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{d_2}}\ar[d]_{(2)} &d_2^!M\ar[d]^{(3)}\\ d_1^*f_*L\ar[r]^{(4)}&h_*c_1^*L\ar[r]^{h_*u}&h_*c_2^*L \ar[r]^{h_*\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}}&h_*c_2^!L\ar[r]^{(5)}&d_2^!f_*L,} \]

where (1) and (3) are given by the adjunction arrow \(M\longrightarrow f_*L\), (2) by the adjunction arrow \(d_2^*M\longrightarrow h_*h^*(d_2^*M)=h_*c_2^*L\), (4) by the base change arrow, and (5) is the canonical isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.3). By definition, \(v'\) is the composite of the upper line. It follows on the other hand from the interpretation of the direct image of a correspondence (III 3.5 b), 3.7) that \(f_*u'\) is the composite of the lower line. It thus suffices to prove the commutativity of the squares \(A\) and \(B\). That of \(A\) follows at once from the definition of the base change arrow. As for that of \(B\), it follows from the fact that, taking account of the tor-independence hypothesis, \(\operatorname{Tr}_{d_2}\) is compatible with base change by \(f\) (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3.3), whence the conclusion.
Applying 6.19, we deduce from it:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.22. — Under the hypotheses of 6.21, we assume that \(f_*f^*M\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}({}_{\Lambda[G]}Y)\), and that the adjunction arrow \(M\longrightarrow f_*f^*M\) induces an isomorphism \(M\xrightarrow{\sim}R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]} (\Lambda,f_*f^*M)\). Then we have
\[ \tag{6.22.1} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(v')= \sum_{g\in G_\natural}\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]} (f_*((f^*v)'))(g^{-1}). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.23. — Under the hypotheses of 6.21, we assume given in addition an open immersion \(i:V\longrightarrow Y\) such that \((fc_1)^{-1}(V)\) is connected, that \(d_1^{-1}(V)\subset d_2^{-1}(V)\), and that \(f\) induces a principal Galois covering with group \(G\), \(U=f^{-1}(V)\xrightarrow{f_V}V\). We assume moreover that \(M\) is of the form \(M=i_!E\), where \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(V)\), and that we have an isomorphism \(\alpha:f_V^*E\xrightarrow{\sim}\Lambda_X\otimes E_o\) of \(D(G,X)\), with \(E_o\in\operatorname{ob}D(\Lambda[G])\), perfect over \(\Lambda\). We are given \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*M,d_2^*M)\) as in 6.21, and we denote by \(v_o\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(E_o,E_o)\) the homomorphism defined, thanks to \(\alpha\), by \(f^*v|_{c_1^{-1}(U)}\). Then, if \(\underline c_{U,X}\) denotes the correspondence defined in 6.14.3, we have (with the notation 6.20.2)
\[ \tag{6.23.1} \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(v')= \sum_{g\in G_\natural}\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]} (f_*\underline c_{U,X})(g^{-1})\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gv_o). \]

(Note that, thanks to the central character of \(\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda\), the second member does not depend on the choice of \(\alpha\)).

By the projection formula \(f_*f^*M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M\xrightarrow{\sim}f_*f^*M\) and 6.15, \(M\) satisfies the hypotheses of 6.22. On the other hand, \(\alpha\) defines an isomorphism \(f^*M\xrightarrow{\sim}\Lambda_{U,X}\otimes E_o\) by which \((f^*v)'\) identifies with the external tensor product \(\underline c_{U,X}\otimes v_o\). By the projection formula, \(f_*f^*M\) thus identifies with \(f_*(\Lambda_{U,X})\otimes E_o\), and \(f_*((f^*v)')\) with the external tensor product \(f_*\underline c_{U,X}\otimes v_o\). For \(g\in G\), the (\(Z_g\)-equivariant) correspondence \(gf_*((f^*v)')\) thus identifies with \(gf_*\underline c_{U,X}\otimes gv_o\). Since, by 6.10.4, we have
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*((f^*v)'))(g^{-1}) =\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gf_*((f^*v)'))(e), \]
it suffices, taking account of 6.22.1, to prove the formula
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gf_*\underline c_{U,X}\otimes gv_o)(e) =\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(f_*\underline c_{U,X})(g^{-1}) \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gv_o), \]
or, what comes to the same thing, by 6.10.4,
\[ \tag{6.23.2} \operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gf_*\underline c_{U,X}\otimes gv_o)(e) =\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[Z_g]}(gf_*\underline c_{U,X})(e) \operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gv_o). \]
Replacing \(G\) by \(Z_g\) if necessary, we may assume that \(g=e\), and, returning to the definition of the traces, we see that it suffices to establish the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.23.3. — (cf. (SGA 41/2 Rapport 4.7)). Let \(P\) be a \(G_V\)-module locally a direct factor of a free module of finite type, \(P'=i_!P\), and \(Q\) a complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules, perfect over \(\Lambda\). Then \(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q\), considered as an object of \(D(\Lambda[G]^V)\) via the diagonal action of \(G\), is perfect over \(\Lambda[G]\), and we have a commutative square
\[ \tag{*} \xymatrix{ \delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\Lambda[G]}(p_1^*P',p_2^!P') \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(Q,Q) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)}&K_Y\ar@{=}[d]\\ \delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\Lambda[G]} (p_1^*P'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q,p_2^!P'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) \ar[r]^{(3)}&K_Y,} \]

where \(\delta:Y\longrightarrow Y\times_S Y\) is the diagonal, (1) is the tensor product of the arrows

\[ \delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\Lambda[G]}(p_1^*P',p_2^!P') \xrightarrow{6.5.3}K\Lambda[G]_{Y,M}\xrightarrow{x\mapsto x(e)}K_Y \]

and

\[ R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(Q,Q) \xrightarrow{\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda}\Lambda, \]

(2) is the arrow deduced from the external tensor product by restriction along the diagonal \(\Lambda[G]\longrightarrow\Lambda[G]\otimes\Lambda[G]\), and (3) is the composite of 6.5.3 (defined since \(P'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q\in\operatorname{ob} D_{ctf}(\Lambda[G]^Y)\)) and of \(x\longmapsto x(e)\)(1).

The first assertion is obvious, for we reduce to \(P=\Lambda[G]_V\), and \(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda Q\) is then isomorphic to the tensor product of \(P\) by \(Q\) considered as equipped with the trivial action of \(G\). For the second, let us note first that, by 6.5.4, 6.5.5, we have

\[ \delta^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\Lambda[G]}(p_1^*P',p_2^!P') \xrightarrow{\sim}D_{\Lambda[G]}(i_!P) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}i_!P \xrightarrow{\sim}i_!\bigl(D_{\Lambda[G]}P \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}P\bigr), \]
so that we are reduced to checking that the square induced by (*) on \(V\) commutes. We may therefore assume that \(V=Y\). The square (*) is then written

\[ \tag{**} \xymatrix{ R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(P,K_Y\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}P) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(Q,Q) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)}&K_Y\ar@{=}[d]\\ R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q, K_Y\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) \ar[r]^{(3)}&K_Y;} \]

where (2) is the external tensor product, (1) is given by \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(-)(e)\otimes\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda\) (traces in the sense of no. 5), (3) by \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda[G]}(-)(e)\). By descent, we reduce to \(P=\Lambda[G]_Y\), and, by the remark at the beginning, we may then assume that \(G\) acts trivially on \(Q\). The assertion is then an immediate consequence of 5.12.5.
If \(\Lambda\) is killed by a power of a prime number \(\ell\ne p\), one can rewrite the second member of 6.23.1 in the form
\[ \sum_{g\in G_\natural}\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]} (f_*\underline c_{U,U,X})(g^{-1})\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda(gv_o), \]

with the notations of 6.17. The formula 6.17.1 shows that the local terms defined by Grothendieck in (XII 6.2) coincide with the local terms of Verdier, at least when the endomorphism \(\omega\) of \(G\) considered in (loc. cit.) is the identity (but, as already observed, this restriction is not serious). If \(Y\) is proper over \(S\), one obtains, by combining 6.23.1 with the Lefschetz formula for \(Y\longrightarrow S\) (III 4.7), a formula which generalizes (XII 6.3) (and a fortiori the Lefschetz formula for Frobenius proved in (SGA 41/2 Rapport) and part I of the present exposé).

footnotes.html:
We agree to let
\(G\) act on the right on the spaces.

of commutative \(\Lambda\)-algebras.

One should be able to replace
\(P\) by a perfect complex (over \(\Lambda[G]\)) (or even \(P'\) by an object of
\(D_{ctf}(\Lambda[G]^Y)\)).
